| **Text-based (RAPID, KRL, VAL3)** | Structured text | Advanced | ⚠ Optional | Powerful, full kinematics | Steep learning curve |
| **Vision-guided scripting** | Python + OpenCV + ROS | Advanced | ⚠ Optional | Adaptive pick of random objects | Needs camera calibration |

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Cycle Time Calculation

**Problem:** A 4-DOF SCARA robot executes the following motion profile between HOME and PICK:

- Distance HOME → APPROACH (PTP): **400 mm** at 1000 mm/s
- Distance APPROACH → PICK (LIN): **20 mm** at 200 mm/s
- Gripper close time: **0.4 s**
- Distance PICK → RETRACT (LIN): **20 mm** at 200 mm/s
- Distance RETRACT → PLACE (PTP): **600 mm** at 1000 mm/s
- Distance PLACE APPROACH → PLACE (LIN): **25 mm** at 200 mm/s**
- Release time: **0.3 s**
- Distance PLACE → HOME (PTP): **500 mm** at 1000 mm/s

**Derive the total cycle time $T_c$.**

### Step 1 — Decompose into segments

$$
T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5 + t_6 + t_7 + t_8
$$

where each $t_i$ corresponds to a numbered segment above.

### Step 2 — Compute each segment time (constant velocity, ignoring acceleration)

$$
t_1 = \dfrac{d_1}{v_1} = \dfrac{400\,\text{mm}}{1000\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.400\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_2 = \dfrac{d_2}{v_2} = \dfrac{20\,\text{mm}}{200\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.100\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_3 = 0.400\,\text{s} \quad \text{(gripper close)}
$$

$$
t_4 = \dfrac{d_4}{v_4} = \dfrac{20\,\text{mm}}{200\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.100\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_5 = \dfrac{d_5}{v_5} = \dfrac{600\,\text{mm}}{1000\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.600\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_6 = \dfrac{d_6}{v_6} = \dfrac{25\,\text{mm}}{200\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.125\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_7 = 0.300\,\text{s} \quad \text{(release)}
$$

$$
t_8 = \dfrac{d_8}{v_8} = \dfrac{500\,\text{mm}}{1000\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.500\,\text{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum the contributions

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 0.400 + 0.100 + 0.400 + 0.100 + 0.600 + 0.125 + 0.300 + 0.500 \\[4pt]
    &= 2.525\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the throughput

$$
\eta = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600}{2.525} \approx 1425.7\,\text{pph}
$$

### Step 5 — Real-world adjustment

Industrial robots spend **15–25 %** of nominal time in acceleration/deceleration. Adjusted cycle time:

$$
T_c^{\text{adj}} = T_c \times 1.20 \approx 3.03\,\text{s}, \quad \eta^{\text{adj}} \approx 1188\,\text{pph}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever cycle time is asked, always present *two* numbers — the *ideal (kinematic)* and the *adjusted (with realistic accel/decel)*. It demonstrates engineering judgment.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Ladder Logic for a PLC-Mastered Pick-and-Place

A typical KTU lab uses the PLC as the master controller, with the robot as a slave that executes pre-stored routines when triggered.

**Inputs (I):**
- `I0.0` — START push-button (NO)
- `I0.1` — Part-present sensor (photo-eye at pick station)
- `I0.2` — HOME position reached (from robot)
- `I0.3` — Gripper-confirm feedback
- `I0.4` — Emergency stop (NC)

**Outputs (Q):**
- `Q0.0` — Trigger routine "PICK"  (rising edge → robot executes P1)
- `Q0.1` — Trigger routine "PLACE" (rising edge → robot executes P2)
- `Q0.2` — Gripper open/close command (1 = close)
- `Q0.3` — Cycle-complete lamp

**Ladder Rung Logic (Conceptual Walk-through):**

| Rung | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| R1 | `I0.0` AND `I0.1` AND NOT `I0.4` AND NOT `M0.1` (busy) | Set `M0.0` (cycle start latch) |
| R2 | `M0.0` AND `I0.2` (robot at HOME) | `Q0.0 = 1` (send PICK pulse) |
| R3 | `I0.3` (gripper closed & confirmed) | `Q0.1 = 1` (send PLACE pulse) AND `Q0.2 = 1` (hold gripper) |
| R4 | `Q0.1` rising edge + timer 1.0 s | `Q0.2 = 0` (release gripper) |
| R5 | Robot returns HOME (`I0.2`) | `Q0.3 = 1` (lamp), reset `M0.0`, increment counter `C1` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important for KTU Viva:** Explain *why* the Emergency Stop (`I0.4`) is checked at *every* rung. A safety contact must be NC (normally closed) so that a wire break automatically stops the machine (fail-safe principle).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pick-and-Place Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HMI[Operator HMI Panel] --> PLC[Master PLC CPU]
    SENS[Conveyor Photo Eye Sensor] --> PLC
    PB[Start Push Button] --> PLC
    ESTOP[Emergency Stop NC] --> PLC
    PLC -->|Digital Out Q0.0| RC[Robot Controller]
    PLC -->|Digital Out Q0.1| RC
    RC -->|Feedback I0.2| PLC
    RC -->|Feedback I0.3| PLC
    RC --> DRV[Servo Drive Pack]
    DRV --> M1[Joint 1 Servo]
    DRV --> M2[Joint 2 Servo]
    DRV --> M3[Joint 3 Servo]
    DRV --> M4[Joint 4 Servo]
    M1 --> ARM[Robot Arm Linkage]
    M2 --> ARM
    M3 --> ARM
    M4 --> ARM
    ARM --> GRP[Pneumatic Gripper End Effector]
    GRP --> OBJ[Workpiece Pick and Place]
    PLC --> LAMP[Cycle Complete Indicator Lamp]
```

## 4.2 Sequential Flow Diagram of a Pick-and-Place Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power ON and System Init] --> B[HOME All Axes]
    B --> C{Part Present at Pick Station?}
    C -->|No| C
    C -->|Yes| D[Move to APPROACH above PICK]
    D --> E[Linear Move Down to PICK POINT]
    E --> F[Close Gripper and Dwell 0.5 s]
    F --> G{Grip Confirm Feedback OK?}
    G -->|No| H[Alarm and Stop]
    G -->|Yes| I[Linear Move Up to RETRACT]
    I --> J[PTP Move to APPROACH above PLACE]
    J --> K[Linear Move Down to PLACE POINT]
    K --> L[Open Gripper and Dwell 0.3 s]
    L --> M[Linear Move Up to RETRACT above PLACE]
    M --> N[Return to HOME Position]
    N --> O[Increment Cycle Counter]
    O --> C
    H --> P[Operator Reset Required]
    P --> A
```

## 4.3 Robot Program Module / Routine Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MAIN[MAIN ROUTINE] --> INIT[Init and Home Subroutine]
    INIT --> LOOP[Cycle Loop]
    LOOP --> WAIT[Wait for Start Signal]
    WAIT --> PICKRT[PICK Routine Module]
    PICKRT --> PLACERT[PLACE Routine Module]
    PLACERT --> LOG[Log Cycle Count and Time]
    LOG --> LOOP
    PICKRT --> P1[Approach Pick]
    P1 --> P2[Descend to Pick]
    P2 --> P3[Actuate Gripper]
    PLACERT --> Q1[Ascend from Pick]
    Q1 --> Q2[Transport to Place]
    Q2 --> Q3[Descend at Place]
    Q3 --> Q4[Release Gripper]
    Q4 --> Q5[Ascend and Return Home]
```

## 4.4 I/O Handshaking Sequence (Timing Diagram in Block Form)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PLC
    participant RBT as Robot Controller
    participant GRP as Gripper
    participant SEN as Sensor

    PLC->>RBT: Q0.0 = 1 (PICK trigger)
    RBT->>RBT: Move to PICK
    RBT->>GRP: Close gripper
    GRP-->>RBT: Grip closed
    RBT-->>PLC: I0.3 = 1 (feedback)
    PLC->>RBT: Q0.1 = 1 (PLACE trigger)
    RBT->>RBT: Move to PLACE
    RBT->>GRP: Open gripper
    SEN-->>PLC: I0.1 = 0 (part dropped)
    RBT-->>PLC: I0.2 = 1 (HOME reached)
    PLC->>PLC: Increment counter, lamp ON
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMRL607]`
**Define the term "pick-and-place operation" in the context of industrial robotics. List the four main sub-operations involved.**

**Model Answer:**
A *pick-and-place operation* is a discrete-event manipulation task in which a robotic arm is programmed to grasp a workpiece from one location and release it at another, forming the basic unit of automated material handling.

The four main sub-operations are:
1. **Approach** — Move the end-effector to a point above the object.
2. **Grasp** — Actuate the gripper to hold the object.
3. **Transport** — Move the held object along a controlled path to the place location.
4. **Release** — De-energize the gripper and retract.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMRL607]`
**What is a teach pendant? How is it used in programming pick-and-place routines?**

**Model Answer:**
A *teach pendant* is a hand-held, ergonomically designed control unit connected to the robot controller via a cable (or wireless link). It has a keypad, an emergency stop button, and a display.

In **pick-and-place** programming, the pendant is used in **lead-through / teach mode**:
- The operator manually jogs the robot to each desired position (HOME, APPROACH, PICK, RETRACT, PLACE APPROACH, PLACE).
- Each position is *recorded* and assigned a label (P1, P2, ...).
- The recorded points are then sequenced into a program with motion-type (PTP / LIN) and speed parameters assigned to each motion block.
- After teaching, the program is *played back* in automatic mode for verification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal-Choice Format:** KTU ESE Part B offers a choice between two questions of equal marks. Two alternatives are provided below.

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PCMRL607]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a PLC-controlled robotic pick-and-place cell**. Label all major components and the signal flow between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cartesian robot must execute the following pick-and-place cycle. Compute the **total cycle time** and the **throughput in parts per hour**. Comment on whether a 20 % safety margin for acceleration changes your cell-design decision. **(7 marks)**

| Segment | Distance (mm) | Type | Speed (mm/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| HOME → APPROACH (PICK) | 500 | PTP | 800 |
| APPROACH → PICK | 30 | LIN | 150 |
| Gripper close | — | Dwell | — |
| PICK → RETRACT | 30 | LIN | 150 |
| RETRACT → APPROACH (PLACE) | 700 | PTP | 1000 |
| APPROACH → PLACE | 40 | LIN | 200 |
| Gripper open | — | Dwell | — |
| PLACE → HOME | 600 | PTP | 800 |

*Assume gripper close = 0.5 s, gripper open = 0.3 s.*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A PLC-controlled robotic pick-and-place cell consists of:

1. **Operator HMI / Push-button station** — Start, Stop, Emergency, Mode selector.
2. **Master PLC (CPU + Digital I/O modules)** — Sequencer, latches, timers, counters.
3. **Robot controller (RC)** — Executes pre-stored motion routines; reports HOME & gripper feedback.
4. **Servo drive pack** — Receives pulse/direction or analog ±10 V from RC; drives the joint motors.
5. **Robot manipulator** (4-DOF or 6-DOF) with **end-effector (pneumatic gripper / vacuum cup)**.
6. **Sensors** — Part-present photo-eye, gripper-confirm proximity switch, HOME limit switch.
7. **Actuators & pneumatics** — 5/2 solenoid valve for gripper, FRL unit, compressor.

**Signal flow:**
- *Forward:* HMI → PLC (digital IN) → PLC CPU → RC (trigger pulses) → Servo drives → Motors.
- *Feedback:* Sensors → RC → PLC (digital IN) → HMI indication.

A clean block diagram of the architecture is provided in **Section 4.1** above and may be redrawn in the answer book for full marks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute individual segment times.**

$$
t_1 = \frac{500}{800} = 0.625\,\text{s},\quad
t_2 = \frac{30}{150} = 0.200\,\text{s},\quad
t_3 = 0.500\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_4 = \frac{30}{150} = 0.200\,\text{s},\quad
t_5 = \frac{700}{1000} = 0.700\,\text{s},\quad
t_6 = \frac{40}{200} = 0.200\,\text{s}
$$

$$
t_7 = 0.300\,\text{s},\quad
t_8 = \frac{600}{800} = 0.750\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total cycle time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 0.625 + 0.200 + 0.500 + 0.200 + 0.700 + 0.200 + 0.300 + 0.750 \\
    &= 3.475\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Throughput.**

$$
\eta = \frac{3600}{3.475} \approx 1035.97 \approx 1036\,\text{pph}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 20 % safety margin.**

$$
T_c^{\text{adj}} = 3.475 \times 1.20 = 4.170\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\eta^{\text{adj}} = \frac{3600}{4.170} \approx 863.3\,\text{pph}
$$

**Comment:**
A 20 % margin (typical for trapezoidal S-curve profiles with accel/decel) reduces throughput from ~1036 pph to ~863 pph. For high-volume consumer goods (e.g., FMCG bottles), this drop is critical and may force a redesign (faster robot, dual gripper, or parallel mechanism). For low-volume industrial parts, the conservative number should be used in capacity-planning quotations.

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PCMRL607]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain **three different methods of programming an industrial robot** for a pick-and-place task, comparing their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a **PLC ladder-logic program** (input/output list + minimum 5 rungs) to control the following sequence: a part arrives on a conveyor at sensor `I0.1`; the robot is triggered to pick (`Q0.0`); after gripper-confirm feedback `I0.2` arrives, the robot is triggered to place (`Q0.1`); the gripper is released (`Q0.2 = 0`); when robot returns HOME (`I0.3`), a cycle-complete lamp `Q0.3` glows for 2 s. Include an Emergency Stop at `I0.0`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Method | Description | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Teach Pendant (Lead-Through)** | Operator physically moves the arm or jogs it via buttons, records each point. | Simple, no code; ideal for small batches and KTU lab use. | Time-consuming, operator-dependent, no offline editing. |
| **2. Offline Programming (OLP)** | Program written in PC software (RobotStudio, RoboDK), then downloaded. | No production downtime; complex paths can be tested in simulation. | Needs accurate cell calibration; tool-frame & base-frame setup critical. |
| **3. PLC / Text-based Structured Programming** | Program written in RAPID, KRL, VAL3, or ladder/FBD on a master PLC. | Full flexibility, I/O handshaking, scalable for multi-robot cells. | Requires trained programmer; longer development cycle. |

**Conclusion for pick-and-place:** Teach pendant is preferred for academic / low-mix labs; OLP for medium-volume production; PLC/structured text for high-volume, sensor-rich, multi-axis cells.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**I/O List:**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `I0.0` | Emergency Stop (NC) | Input |
| `I0.1` | Part-present sensor (photo-eye) | Input |
| `I0.2` | Gripper-confirm feedback | Input |
| `I0.3` | Robot HOME position reached | Input |
| `Q0.0` | Trigger robot PICK routine | Output |
| `Q0.1` | Trigger robot PLACE routine | Output |
| `Q0.2` | Gripper close (1) / open (0) | Output |
| `Q0.3` | Cycle-complete lamp | Output |
| `M0.0` | Cycle-running latch | Memory |
| `T1` | 2-second ON-delay timer | Timer |
| `C1` | Cycle counter | Counter |

**Ladder Rungs (conceptual — full marks if drawn in exam):**

- **Rung 1 — Emergency guard:** If `NOT I0.0` (E-stop pressed), then `M0.0` is reset (cycle abort).
- **Rung 2 — Start cycle:** If `I0.0` AND `I0.1` AND `NOT M0.0`, then latch `M0.0`.
- **Rung 3 — Trigger PICK:** If `M0.0` AND `I0.3` (robot at HOME), then `Q0.0` pulse (use rising-edge one-shot) AND `Q0.2 = 1` (close gripper after arrival — controlled by Rung 4).
- **Rung 4 — Wait for grip-confirm:** If `M0.0` AND `I0.2`, then `Q0.1` rising-edge pulse (trigger PLACE).
- **Rung 5 — Release & lamp:** If `Q0.1` rising edge → start `T1` (2 s). `T1` running → `Q0.2 = 0` (open gripper). `T1` done → `Q0.3 = 1` AND `C1 = C1 + 1` AND reset `M0.0`.

> **Validation logic in plain English:** The PLC will not start a new pick unless the robot is HOME (`I0.3`), an object is present (`I0.1`), and no emergency is active. The gripper is *kept closed* during transport (`Q0.2 = 1`) and is *released* only after the place-pulse + 2-second timer has elapsed, ensuring the part is settled.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in pick-and-place questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the APPROACH / RETRACT offset point.** Direct move from HOME to PICK POINT at full PTP speed = collision risk. Examiners look for these *two* intermediate points. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing PTP and LIN motion types.** PTP is fast but non-linear; LIN preserves path. Pick/Place must use LIN for the final descent; examiners deduct marks if PTP is used at the pick point. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Ignoring cycle time realism.** Always state both *ideal* and *adjusted (with acceleration margin)* times.
> 4. **Ladder logic without an Emergency Stop rung.** Any PLC program without a hardwired E-stop is considered unsafe and will be marked down. **(–2 to –3 marks)**
> 5. **Omitting the dwell time after gripper actuation.** The dwell is mandatory for pneumatic systems.
> 6. **Wrong polarity of the E-stop contact** (using NO instead of NC). NC is the *fail-safe* convention.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- ✅ A pick-and-place routine needs **at least 6 points**: HOME, APPROACH-PICK, PICK, RETRACT-PICK, APPROACH-PLACE, PLACE, RETRACT-PLACE.
- ✅ Use **PTP** for high-speed transit; **LIN** for precise approach/retract and final descent/ascent.
- ✅ Always include an **APPROACH OFFSET (Z + 10–50 mm)** above the pick and place points.
- ✅ Include a **Dwell (0.3–1.0 s)** after gripper close/open commands.
- ✅ The **PLC and robot must handshake** using digital I/O bits for START, GRIP-CONFIRM, HOME, and CYCLE-DONE.
- ✅ **Emergency Stop must be NC (fail-safe)** and must reset the cycle latch in the PLC.
- ✅ **Cycle time** = sum of motion times + dwell times. **Throughput** = 3600 / T_c.
- ✅ Use a **20 % safety margin** on cycle time to account for acceleration/deceleration in real cells.
- ✅ Three programming methods — **Teach Pendant, Offline Programming, Structured Text / Ladder** — each with distinct trade-offs.
- ✅ Always use **ladder logic conventions** (power flow left → right, NC for safety, NO for start).
- ✅ In KTU lab record, mention the **I/O list, ladder diagram, and timing calculation** — all three earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors for automotive applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL607 - MECHATRONICS SYSTEMS & PLC LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors for automotive applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — SENSORS FOR AUTOMOTIVE APPLICATIONS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Sensors (KTU 2024 Definition):** Automotive sensors are electro-mechanical or solid-state transducer devices that continuously monitor critical physical, chemical, and dynamic parameters of a vehicle — such as **temperature**, **pressure**, **position**, **speed**, **acceleration**, **gas concentration**, and **proximity** — and convert these into standardized electrical signals (voltage, current, frequency, or digital protocols like **CAN**, **LIN**, or **SPI**) for the Engine Control Unit (ECU), Transmission Control Unit (TCU), or Body Control Modules (BCM) to make real-time actuation decisions.

In the context of the **PCMRL607 Lab** (Mechatronics Systems & PLC Lab), the automotive sensor experiment is designed to expose students to the **signal-conditioning chain** — Transducer → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → PLC/Microcontroller — and to verify the **transfer characteristics** of sensors used in modern Electronic Control Units (ECUs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Five Senses" of a Car

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Just as a human driver uses five senses to drive a car, a modern automobile uses dozens of sensors as its **electronic senses**:
> - **Touch (Pressure/TPMS)** → senses road contact & tire pressure
> - **Smell (Oxygen/NOx Sensor)** → senses exhaust gas chemistry
> - **Sight (Camera/LiDAR/Rain Sensor)** → senses surroundings and weather
> - **Balance (Gyroscope/Accelerometer – IMU)** → senses orientation & stability
> - **Hearing (Knock Sensor/Microphone)** → senses abnormal engine combustion

The **ECU** acts as the **"brain"** that fuses these senses to control actuators like fuel injectors, ignition coils, ABS modulators, and steering motors.

## 1.3 Classification of Automotive Sensors

| Class | Sub-Category | Example Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| **Powertrain Sensors** | Engine & Transmission | MAP, MAF, TPS, CKP, CMP, ECT, Knock |
| **Safety Sensors** | Chassis & Braking | Wheel Speed, Yaw Rate, Steering Angle, Accelerometer |
| **Emission Sensors** | Exhaust After-treatment | Lambda (O₂), NOx, Particulate Matter |
| **Comfort/Body Sensors** | Cabin & Body | Rain-Light, Ultrasonic, Seat Occupancy, Fuel Level |
| **ADAS Sensors** | Advanced Driver Assist | Radar, LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic, IMU |
| **Communication** | Bus-level sensing | LIN, CAN, FlexRay transducers |

## 1.4 Syllabus-Mandated Learning Outcomes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After completing this experiment, the KTU 2024 student **must be able to**:
> 1. **CO1:** Identify and classify sensors used in modern automotive Electronic Control Units (ECUs).
> 2. **CO2:** Interpret the static and dynamic characteristics (sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, response time) of automotive sensors.
> 3. **CO3:** Interface sensors with **PLC / DAQ / Microcontroller** and validate the output response curve.
> 4. **CO4:** Analyze sensor signal-conditioning circuits (amplification, filtering, linearization) used in mechatronic subsystems.
> 5. **CO5:** Evaluate the suitability of a sensor for a given automotive application based on operating range, accuracy, and environmental ruggedness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Major Automotive Sensors

### 2.1.1 Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor

- **Type:** Hot-wire anemometer (thermal mass flow).
- **Working Principle:** A heated platinum wire is maintained at a constant temperature **130 °C above** incoming air. As air mass flow increases, more heat is carried away, requiring greater heating current.
- **Output:** Analog voltage **0 V to 5 V**, scaled to the air-mass range.
- **Equation:**
> **ṁ = (V · K) / R_h** where **V** is bridge output voltage, **K** is calibration constant, **R_h** is hot-wire resistance.

### 2.1.2 Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor

- **Type:** Piezoresistive / Capacitive silicon diaphragm.
- **Working Principle:** A Wheatstone-bridge piezoresistor on a silicon diaphragm flexes as intake-manifold vacuum changes.
- **Output:** Analog **0 V – 5 V** corresponding to **10 kPa – 115 kPa**.
- **Equation:**
> **V_out = V_s · [ΔR / (4R)] · GF** where **GF** is the gauge factor (~100–150 for Si).

### 2.1.3 Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)

- **Type:** Rotary potentiometric / Hall-effect non-contact.
- **Working Principle:** Mechanical linkage rotates a magnet or wiper. The wiper position on a resistance track produces a **ratiometric voltage**.
- **Output:** **0 V** (idle, ~6 %) → **5 V** (WOT, ~94 %).
- **Equation:**
> **V_out = V_ref · (θ / θ_max)** where **θ** is the throttle angle.

### 2.1.4 Oxygen (Lambda) Sensor

- **Type:** Zirconia (ZrO₂) galvanic cell.
- **Working Principle:** At **350 °C +**, oxygen ions migrate across the zirconia electrolyte creating Nernst voltage between exhaust gas and reference air.
- **Output:** **0.1 V (lean)** ↔ **0.9 V (rich)** with step at **λ = 1** (stoichiometric).
- **Equation:**
> **E = (RT / 4F) · ln(P_O₂,ref / P_O₂,exh)**

### 2.1.5 Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)

- **Type:** Variable Reluctance (VR) or Hall-effect.
- **Working Principle:** A 36-minus-1 tooth reluctor wheel passes a pickup coil, inducing an AC voltage proportional to **dΦ/dt**.
- **Output:** AC sine (VR) or **0–5 V square wave** (Hall).
- **Equation:**
> **e = −N · dΦ/dt**

### 2.1.6 Knock Sensor

- **Type:** Piezoelectric (PZT) accelerometer, resonant at **7 kHz**.
- **Working Principle:** Engine knock produces vibrations; piezoelectric crystal converts mechanical stress into charge.
- **Output:** AC millivolt signal fed to ECU's band-pass filter (centered ~7 kHz).

### 2.1.7 Wheel Speed Sensor (ABS)

- **Type:** Variable Reluctance / Hall-effect.
- **Working Principle:** Tone ring teeth modulate magnetic flux; frequency proportional to wheel speed.
- **Output:** AC sine (VR) or **square wave** (Hall).
- **Equation:**
> **f = (N · ω) / (2π)** where **N** is number of teeth, **ω** is angular speed.

### 2.1.8 Ultrasonic Parking Sensor

- **Type:** Piezoelectric ultrasonic transceiver (**40 kHz – 58 kHz**).
- **Working Principle:** Emits burst; receives echo; Time-of-Flight gives distance.
- **Equation:**
> **d = (v_sound · t) / 2** with **v_sound = 343 m/s** at **20 °C**.

### 2.1.9 Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)

- **Type:** Piezoresistive pressure + **RF transmitter (315 MHz / 433 MHz)**.
- **Working Principle:** Battery-powered in-tire module transmits pressure/temperature every **60 s** to a central receiver.

### 2.1.10 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) — Accelerometer & Gyroscope

- **MEMS Accelerometer:** A proof mass suspended by flexure beams; deflection under acceleration changes capacitance.
- **Equation:** **a = (ΔC · k) / m** where **m** is proof mass.
- **MEMS Gyroscope:** Coriolis force on a vibrating mass when rotated.
- **Equation:** **F_c = 2m (Ω × v)**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Piezoelectric Output | **V = g · d · t / C** | V | g = piezo constant |
| 2 | Nernst (O₂ Sensor) | **E = (RT/4F) · ln(P_ref/P_exh)** | V | T = 873 K |
| 3 | Strain Gauge | **ε = ΔL/L**, **GF = (ΔR/R)/ε** | dimensionless | GF = 2.0–200 |
| 4 | Piezoresistive | **ΔR/R = GF · ε** | dimensionless | Si: 100–150 |
| 5 | RTD (Pt100) | **R_T = R_0 [1 + α(T − T_0)]** | Ω | α = 0.00385 /°C |
| 6 | Thermistor (NTC) | **R_T = R_0 · e^(B(1/T − 1/T_0))** | Ω | B = 3435 K |
| 7 | Ultrasonic Distance | **d = (v · t) / 2** | m | v = 343 m/s |
| 8 | Doppler Radar | **f_d = 2 · v · f_0 / c** | Hz | f_0 = 76 GHz |
| 9 | CKP Frequency | **f = N · RPM / 60** | Hz | N = 60 − 2 |
| 10 | Sensitivity | **S = ΔV_out / ΔX** | V/unit |  |
| 11 | Linearity | **%NL = (ΔY_max / Y_FS) · 100** | % | ≤ 1 % |
| 12 | Hysteresis | **%H = (Y_up − Y_down) / Y_FS · 100** | % | ≤ 0.5 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units in formulas:** Inside the markdown table above, all absolute-value bars and division slashes are rendered as plain LaTeX commands (`/`) to comply with the **NO VERTICAL PIPE** protocol. When copying to a notebook, replace `/` with `\vert` if absolute value is intended.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Emission Compliance:** Lambda and NOx sensors enable Euro-6 / BS-VI compliance.
> - **Fuel Economy:** MAF + MAP + TPS optimize air-fuel ratio, improving km/L by **10–15 %**.
> - **Active Safety:** IMU + wheel-speed sensor enable **ABS**, **ESC**, and **rollover mitigation**.
> - **ADAS:** Radar/LiDAR/Ultrasonic fusion enables **AEB** (Autonomous Emergency Braking).
> - **Driver Comfort:** Rain-light sensors automatically activate wipers/headlamps.

## 2.4 Static & Dynamic Characteristics to Verify in the Lab

- **Static:** Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability, Drift, Dead-band.
- **Dynamic:** Rise time, Settling time, Bandwidth, Natural frequency, Damping ratio.
- **Environmental:** Operating temperature (**−40 °C to +125 °C** for under-hood), vibration tolerance (**20 g RMS**), EMI/EMC immunity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Nernst Equation for Lambda (Oxygen) Sensor

The zirconia oxygen sensor behaves as a **solid electrolyte galvanic cell**. The two half-reactions are:

$$
\text{Cathode (reference):} \quad O_2 + 4e^- \rightarrow 2O^{2-}
$$

$$
\text{Anode (exhaust):} \quad 2O^{2-} \rightarrow O_2 + 4e^-
$$

The Gibbs free energy change per mole of O₂ is:

$$
\Delta G = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{P_{O_2,\text{exh}}}\right)
$$

Equating electrical work (4 electrons per O₂) to chemical work:

$$
-nFE = \Delta G \quad \Rightarrow \quad -4FE = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{P_{O_2,\text{exh}}}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{P_{O_2,\text{exh}}}\right)}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation at T = 873 K (600 °C):**

$$
R = 8.314 \ \text{J/(mol·K)}, \quad F = 96485 \ \text{C/mol}
$$

$$
\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{8.314 \times 873}{4 \times 96485} = 0.01882 \ \text{V}
$$

If the air-fuel mixture is **stoichiometric (λ = 1)**, the partial-pressure ratio ≈ 10⁴, giving:

$$
E = 0.01882 \times \ln(10^4) = 0.01882 \times 9.2103 = 0.173 \ \text{V}
$$

In practice, the Nernst cell is connected in series with a **Heater + Pump-cell** and the output is amplified to the characteristic **0.1 V (lean) ↔ 0.9 V (rich)** transition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Time-of-Flight (Ultrasonic Distance)

Distance in an ultrasonic parking sensor is computed from echo round-trip time:

$$
d = v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \frac{t}{2}
$$

The factor of **2** arises because **t** is the *round-trip* time (transmit + receive). Speed of sound in air as a function of temperature:

$$
v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad (\text{m/s, T in °C})
$$

**Numerical example:** Echo returned in **t = 5.85 ms** at **T = 20 °C**:

$$
v = 331 + 0.6 \times 20 = 343 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
d = 343 \times \frac{5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 343 \times 2.925 \times 10^{-3} = 1.003 \ \text{m}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Wheel-Speed Sensor Frequency

A 48-tooth tone ring rotates at wheel angular speed **ω (rad/s)**. Each tooth produces one AC cycle.

$$
f = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{N \cdot \text{RPM}}{60}
$$

**Numerical example:** N = 48 teeth, vehicle speed = 60 km/h, wheel circumference = 2 m.

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{16.67 \ \text{m/s}}{0.318 \ \text{m}} = 52.4 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
f = \frac{48 \times 52.4}{2\pi} = \frac{2515}{6.283} = 400.3 \ \text{Hz}
$$

The ECU counts pulses per second to compute **vehicle speed** and detects **rapid frequency decay** as a wheel-lock signature for ABS intervention.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Sensitivity & Linearity (Lab Verification)

For a **potentiometric TPS** with reference voltage V_ref = 5 V and full angle θ_max = 90°:

$$
S = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta \theta} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{\theta_{\text{max}}} = \frac{5}{90} = 0.0556 \ \text{V/°}
$$

**Linearity error** at any angle θ:

$$
\%NL = \frac{|V_{\text{ideal}}(\theta) - V_{\text{actual}}(\theta)|}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision Table

| Application | Required Range | Bandwidth | Output | Recommended Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intake air mass | 5–500 kg/h | DC–100 Hz | 0–5 V | Hot-wire MAF |
| Manifold vacuum | 10–115 kPa | DC–1 kHz | 0–5 V | Piezoresistive MAP |
| Throttle angle | 0°–90° | DC–100 Hz | 0–5 V | Hall TPS |
| Engine knock detect | 5–15 kHz | 5–10 kHz | mV AC | PZT resonant |
| Wheel speed (ABS) | 0–250 km/h | 0–5 kHz | AC/square | VR / Hall |
| Cabin proximity | 0.2–2.5 m | 40–58 kHz burst | TOF | Ultrasonic |
| Tire pressure | 0–5 bar | < 1 Hz | RF 433 MHz | Piezoresistive + RF |
| Exhaust O₂ | λ 0.7–1.3 | 0.1–1 Hz | 0.1–0.9 V | ZrO₂ Lambda |
| Vehicle yaw rate | ±100 °/s | DC–50 Hz | 0–5 V analog | MEMS Gyro |
| Crash deceleration | ±50 g | DC–1 kHz | Analog | MEMS Accel. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Quantity] --> B[Sensor Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Amplifier]
    C --> D[Anti-Alias Filter]
    D --> E[ADC 12 bit]
    E --> F[ECU Microcontroller]
    F --> G{Decision Logic}
    G --> H[Actuator Driver]
    H --> I[Vehicle Actuator]
    F --> J[CAN Bus]
    J --> K[Dashboard Display]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A → B: Physical variable (e.g., pressure) acts on the transducer.
- B → C: Wheatstone-bridge / piezo output is amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier (INA128)**.
- C → D: Low-pass filter (cut-off 1 kHz) removes high-frequency noise.
- D → E: 12-bit ADC (e.g., MCP3202) digitizes the signal.
- E → F: MCU (e.g., STM32 / dsPIC33) runs the control algorithm.
- F → G/H: Decision logic drives the actuator (injector, ABS valve, etc.).
- F → J/K: Diagnostic and dashboard data shared over **CAN bus** at **500 kbps**.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Powertrain Sensor Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EngineBay["Engine Bay Sensors"]
        MAF[MAF Sensor]
        MAP[MAP Sensor]
        TPS[TPS Sensor]
        ECT[Engine Coolant Temp]
        CKP[Crankshaft Position]
        CMP[Camshaft Position]
        KNK[Knock Sensor]
    end

    subgraph Chassis["Chassis Sensors"]
        WSS[Wheel Speed x4]
        SAS[Steering Angle Sensor]
        YAW[Yaw Rate Gyro]
        ACC[Longitudinal Accel]
    end

    subgraph Emission["Emission Sensors"]
        LAM[Lambda O2 Sensor]
        NOX[NOx Sensor]
        DPF[DPF Pressure]
    end

    subgraph ADAS["ADAS Sensor Suite"]
        RAD[77 GHz Radar]
        LID[LiDAR]
        CAM[Forward Camera]
        USN[Ultrasonic x12]
    end

    ECU[Engine ECU]
    BCM[Body Control Module]
    ABSU[ABS ESC ECU]
    ADU[ADAS Domain Controller]

    EngineBay --> ECU
    Chassis --> ABSU
    Emission --> ECU
    ADAS --> ADU
    ECU --> BCM
    ECU --> ADU
    ABSU --> ADU
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Lab Experiment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Connect Sensor Module] --> P2[Apply Reference Stimulus]
    P2 --> P3[Record Output via DAQ / PLC]
    P3 --> P4{Vary Stimulus Step by Step}
    P4 --> P5[Capture Voltage vs Input]
    P5 --> P6[Repeat for Hysteresis Run]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis]
    P7 --> P8[Plot Calibration Curve]
    P8 --> P9[Compare with Datasheet]
    P9 --> P10[Lab Report Submission]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Wheatstone Bridge for Piezoresistive MAP Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vex[V excitation 5V] --- R1[Fixed R1]
    R1 --- nodeA((Node A))
    nodeA --- R2[Active R2 strain gauge]
    R2 --- GND1[Ground]
    Vex --- R3[Fixed R3]
    R3 --- nodeB((Node B))
    nodeB --- R4[Active R4 strain gauge]
    R4 --- GND2[Ground]
    nodeA --- AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier INA128]
    nodeB --- AMP
    AMP --> Vout[Vout to ADC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two active gauges (R2, R4) are mounted on **opposite sides** of the silicon diaphragm. When pressure flexes the diaphragm, R2 increases while R4 decreases — producing a **complementary differential output** that the INA128 amplifies by **G = 1 + 49.4k/R_g**.

---

## 4.5 PLC Interfacing — Sensor-to-PLC Wiring Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Temperature Sensor PT100] -->|RTD wires| TR[Transmitter 4-20 mA]
    S2[Pressure Sensor] -->|0-10V| AI1[PLC Analog Input AI0]
    S3[TPS Sensor] -->|0-5V| AI2[PLC Analog Input AI1]
    S4[Ultrasonic Sensor] -->|0-10V| AI3[PLC Analog Input AI2]
    S5[Speed Sensor Hall] -->|Pulse| DI1[PLC Digital Input DI0]
    TR --> AI4[PLC Analog Input AI3]
    AI1 --> CPU[PLC CPU]
    AI2 --> CPU
    AI3 --> CPU
    AI4 --> CPU
    DI1 --> CPU
    CPU --> OUT1[Indicator Lamp]
    CPU --> OUT2[HMI Display]
    CPU --> OUT3[Buzzer Alarm]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three automotive sensors and state the physical quantity measured by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sensor | Physical Quantity | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **MAF (Mass Air Flow) Sensor** | Mass of air entering the engine (kg/h) | ECU fuel injection control |
| **CKP (Crankshaft Position) Sensor** | Rotational position / speed of crankshaft (° / RPM) | Ignition timing, fuel injection |
| **Lambda (O₂) Sensor** | Oxygen concentration in exhaust gas (λ ratio) | Closed-loop air-fuel control |
| **TPMS (Tire Pressure Sensor)** | Tire air pressure (kPa / bar) | Low-pressure warning |

*Each correct sensor with correct quantity: 1 mark × 3 = 3 marks.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a piezoresistive and a piezoelectric sensor, giving one automotive example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Piezoresistive | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Change in resistance under strain | Charge generation under stress |
| **Output type** | Quasi-DC, requires excitation | AC, self-generating |
| **Frequency response** | 0 – 1 kHz | 100 Hz – 100 kHz |
| **Example (Automotive)** | **MAP Sensor** (Si diaphragm) | **Knock Sensor** (PZT) |

*1 mark for principle difference, 1 mark for output/frequency, 1 mark for examples.*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCMRL607]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a **Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor** based on the hot-wire anemometer principle. Derive the relationship between output voltage and air mass flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two wheel-speed sensors each with **48 teeth** are fitted to a vehicle's rear wheels. The wheels have an effective radius of **0.32 m**. The vehicle is decelerating from **80 km/h to rest in 5 s** uniformly. Calculate the **frequency of the wheel-speed sensor signal at the start and end of braking**, and the **deceleration**. Also identify the sensor type most suited for ABS application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question A)**

**(a) — MAF Sensor Working (7 marks)**

- **Introduction (1 mark):** The MAF sensor measures the mass of intake air to the engine. It is a critical input to the ECU for stoichiometric air-fuel ratio computation.
- **Construction (2 marks):** A thin platinum hot-wire (Rw) is placed in the intake airstream. A temperature-compensating cold-wire (Rk) sits upstream. Both form arms of a **Wheatstone bridge** within a closed control loop.
- **Working Principle (2 marks):** The hot-wire is maintained at a constant temperature differential (ΔT = 130 °C) above the incoming air. As air mass flow (ṁ) increases, more heat is convected away; the control loop increases the bridge current to compensate, raising the output voltage V_out.
- **Derivation (2 marks):**

Heat lost by convection = Electrical power supplied

$$
\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = \frac{V_{\text{out}}^2}{R_w}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \dot{m} = \frac{V_{\text{out}}^2}{R_w \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}
$$

This non-linear **V² ∝ ṁ** relationship is linearized in the ECU via a look-up table.

- **ECU Interface (extra point):** Output **0–5 V analog**; Bosch / Hitachi / Siemens MAF types also provide **digital frequency output** for some manufacturers.

---

**(b) — Wheel Speed Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert vehicle speed to wheel angular speed (1 mark):**

$$
v_1 = 80 \ \text{km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\omega_1 = \frac{v_1}{r} = \frac{22.22}{0.32} = 69.44 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial frequency (1 mark):**

$$
f_1 = \frac{N \cdot \omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{48 \times 69.44}{2\pi} = \frac{3333.3}{6.283} = 530.5 \ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Final frequency at rest (1 mark):** At v₂ = 0, **f₂ = 0 Hz**.

**Step 4 — Deceleration (2 marks):**

$$
a = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{t} = \frac{0 - 22.22}{5} = -4.44 \ \text{m/s}^2 \quad (\approx -0.453g)
$$

**Step 5 — Sensor recommendation (2 marks):**
- **Hall-effect active wheel-speed sensor** is preferred for ABS because:
  1. **DC-coupled square-wave output** (works down to **0 km/h / 0 Hz**) — VR sensors fail below ~5 km/h.
  2. **Direction detection** capability (needed for ESC).
  3. **Better EMI immunity** in modern vehicles.

**Result:** **f₁ = 530.5 Hz**, **f₂ = 0 Hz**, **a = 4.44 m/s²**, **Sensor type: Active Hall-effect**.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank, PCMRL607]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **zirconia-dioxide Lambda (O₂) sensor**. Derive the Nernst equation governing its output voltage. A wide-band sensor operates at **T = 873 K** with reference oxygen partial pressure **P_ref = 21 kPa**. Compute the Nernst voltage when the exhaust O₂ partial pressure is **P_exh = 0.21 kPa**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic parking sensor operates at **f₀ = 40 kHz** in air at **25 °C**. It detects an echo from an obstacle after **t = 12 ms**. Calculate the obstacle distance. If the same sensor is later used at **T = 0 °C**, by what percentage does the measured distance error increase, assuming the ECU still uses the **20 °C calibration constant**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question B)**

**(a) — Lambda Sensor (7 marks)**

- **Construction (2 marks):** A thimble-shaped **zirconia (ZrO₂)** ceramic body stabilized with **yttria (Y₂O₃)** has porous platinum electrodes on inner (reference) and outer (exhaust) surfaces. An integrated heater raises the sensor to **> 350 °C** for ionic conduction.
- **Working (2 marks):** At operating temperature, oxygen ions (O²⁻) migrate from high-PO₂ to low-PO₂ side, producing a galvanic EMF between the electrodes. A **step transition** occurs at **λ = 1** because the O₂ partial pressure drops by **~10⁴** at stoichiometry.
- **Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right)
$$

**Numerical evaluation (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{8.314 \times 873}{4 \times 96485} = 0.01882 \ \text{V}
$$

$$
E = 0.01882 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{21}{0.21}\right) = 0.01882 \times \ln(100)
$$

$$
E = 0.01882 \times 4.6052 = 0.0867 \ \text{V} \approx 86.7 \ \text{mV}
$$

> In practical wide-band sensors, a **pump cell** forces a current to maintain λ = 1, and the **pump current** (not the Nernst voltage directly) becomes the linear output.

---

**(b) — Ultrasonic Distance (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute v_sound at 25 °C (1 mark):**

$$
v_{25} = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 346 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute distance d at 25 °C (2 marks):**

$$
d_{25} = \frac{v_{25} \cdot t}{2} = \frac{346 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.076 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute v_sound at 0 °C (1 mark):**

$$
v_0 = 331 + 0.6 \times 0 = 331 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — True distance at 0 °C (1 mark):**

$$
d_0 = \frac{331 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.986 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage error (2 marks):**

$$
\% \text{Error} = \frac{d_{25} - d_0}{d_0} \times 100 = \frac{2.076 - 1.986}{1.986} \times 100 = 4.53 \%
$$

The ECU reports a distance **4.53 % greater** than the true distance if it does not compensate for temperature — hence production ECU firmware includes a **temperature-compensated v_sound model**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Unit-Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert **km/h → m/s** or **ms → s** is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers. Always show unit conversion as a separate step.
> 2. **Mixing up Piezoelectric vs Piezoresistive:** Piezoelectric = AC, self-generating (Knock). Piezoresistive = DC, needs excitation (MAP). Examiners award 1 full mark for correctly identifying this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight:** Time **t** is round-trip. Distance is **v·t/2**, not **v·t**.
> 4. **Lambda Sensor Nernst Equation:** Students often forget the **factor 4** in denominator. Always write the **half-cell reaction** (4 electrons per O₂) and the **RT/nF** derivation explicitly.
> 5. **Hall vs VR Sensor for ABS:** A VR sensor cannot detect speed below ~5 km/h. Always recommend **Hall-effect (active)** for ABS/ESC — that single sentence can fetch 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the Calibration Curve Plot:** In lab records, examiners deduct marks if the **sensitivity, linearity, and hysteresis values** are not tabulated alongside the plotted curve.
> 7. **Wire-color / PLC analog input configuration:** When interfacing to a PLC, students forget to configure the **analog input as voltage (0–10 V) or current (4–20 mA)** mode in the PLC software. This is a common practical-evaluation trap.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module Experiment 2 — Sensors for Automotive Applications):**

### ✔ Sensor Classifications
- **Powertrain:** MAF, MAP, TPS, CKP, CMP, ECT, Knock
- **Chassis/Safety:** Wheel Speed, Yaw, Steering Angle, Accel (IMU)
- **Emission:** Lambda O₂, NOx, DPF Pressure
- **Comfort/Body:** Rain-Light, Ultrasonic, TPMS, Fuel Level
- **ADAS:** Radar (77 GHz), LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic

### ✔ Core Equations to Memorize
- **Nernst (O₂):** $E = \dfrac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right)$
- **Time-of-Flight (Ultrasonic):** $d = v \cdot t / 2$
- **Wheel Frequency:** $f = N\omega/2\pi = N \cdot RPM/60$
- **MAF (Hot-wire):** $\dot{m} = V_{\text{out}}^2 / (R_w \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T)$
- **RTD (Pt100):** $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$
- **Strain Gauge:** $\Delta R/R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$

### ✔ Signal Conditioning Chain
- **Transducer → Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128) → LPF → 12-bit ADC → MCU → Actuator Driver → CAN/LIN Bus**

### ✔ Lab Static Characteristics
- **Sensitivity** = ΔV/ΔX
- **Linearity** ≤ **±1 %** (Grade-A sensor)
- **Hysteresis** ≤ **±0.5 %** of Full Scale

### ✔ Key Operating Ranges
- **Engine-bay temperature:** **−40 °C to +125 °C**
- **Vibration tolerance:** up to **20 g RMS**
- **Ultrasonic frequency:** **40 – 58 kHz**
- **Automotive radar:** **76 – 77 GHz**
- **TPMS RF:** **315 MHz (US) / 433 MHz (EU)**

### ✔ PLC Interfacing (Lab Procedure)
- Analog sensors → **0–10 V** or **4–20 mA** to PLC **AI0–AI3**
- Hall pulse sensors → **DI0** (high-speed counter input)
- Configure PLC analog input as **voltage / current** mode in software
- Record calibration curve; compute **sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis**

### ✔ Sensor Selection Golden Rule
> **"Choose the sensor that meets the required range, accuracy, and environmental ruggedness, at the lowest cost and smallest package — and which can communicate natively with the ECU/PLC without expensive signal conditioning."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic door opening and closing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL607 - MECHATRONICS SYSTEMS & PLC LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic door opening and closing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Automatic Door Opening and Closing System** is a mechatronic assembly that integrates sensing elements, a programmable logic controller (PLC), signal-conditioning hardware, and electromechanical actuators to perform door actuation without manual intervention. According to the **KTU 2024 Mechatronics Lab syllabus**, this experiment demonstrates the closed-loop control of a translational mechanical load (the door) using sensor feedback, discrete I/O logic in a PLC, and an actuator drive stage.

The system is classified under **Discrete Event Control (DEC)** because the door states are finite, binary, and event-driven (Open / Close / Idle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:**
> A mechatronic system in which an **input sensor** detects the presence of a person/object, sends a logic signal to a **PLC**, which then energizes a **motor/actuator** to drive the door from its current position to the opposite limit, holding it open for a preset dwell time, and reversing motion automatically to return it to the closed position.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mall Sliding Door" Intuition

Imagine the automatic glass doors at the entrance of a shopping mall (e.g., **Lulu Mall, Kochi**). When you walk toward them:

1. You (the *object*) break a **photonic beam** or reflect a **microwave/IR signal** at the entrance.
2. The door *magically* slides open sideways.
3. You walk in.
4. After a few seconds (the **dwell time**), the door slides back and shuts.

In our lab, the "magic" is actually a chain of very well-defined components working in synchrony:

| Mall Analogy | Lab Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Person approaching | Object / target |
| Motion sensor above door | **IR Proximity Sensor / Ultrasonic Sensor** |
| Door control panel | **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** |
| Sliding mechanism motor | **DC Geared Motor / Stepper Motor** |
| Door hitting the wall | **Limit Switch (Open position)** |
| Door fully closed | **Limit Switch (Close position)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The PLC is essentially the *brain*, the sensor is the *eye*, the motor is the *muscle*, and the limit switches are the *proprioceptive nerves* that tell the brain "we've reached the wall, stop pushing!"

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **bolded** as they are standard in KTU exam answers:

- **Standard PLC Scan Time:** Typically **10 ms** to **20 ms** per scan cycle for small modular PLCs (Siemens S7-200, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix, Delta DVP).
- **Standard Sensor Range (IR):** **2 cm to 80 cm** for diffuse reflective type; up to **10 m** for through-beam.
- **Standard Sensor Range (Ultrasonic HC-SR04):** **2 cm to 400 cm** with ±3 mm accuracy.
- **Standard DC Geared Motor RPM:** **30 RPM to 300 RPM** (geared down for torque).
- **Standard Relay Coil Voltage:** **24 V DC** (compatible with PLC output ratings).
- **Standard Ladder Logic Voltage (in KTU lab):** **+24 V DC** as logic HIGH (1).
- **Standard Ladder Logic LOW (0):** **0 V DC** or open contact.
- **Gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (used for force/torque calculations on the door leaf).
- **Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa** (used when pneumatic actuator variant is employed).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Architecture

The automatic door system is a textbook example of a **Sense → Decide → Actuate → Feedback** mechatronic loop. Let us break down each stage:

### Stage 1: Sensing
- An **IR proximity sensor** (or **PIR sensor** for human body IR detection at 8–14 µm wavelength) is mounted on the approach side of the door.
- When an object enters the detection zone (typically 30–100 cm), the sensor output transitions from **logic 0 → logic 1**.
- This signal is wired to a **digital input (DI) terminal** of the PLC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Debouncing Note:** A real sensor has a finite response time (~10–20 ms). The PLC scan must be slower than this to avoid missing pulses.

### Stage 2: Decision (PLC Logic)
- The PLC executes a **ladder logic program** cycle by cycle.
- Ladder logic uses contacts (NO/NC) and coils (outputs) to implement Boolean equations.
- The core logic equation is:
  - **Motor Forward (Open) = Sensor ⋅ NOT(Limit_Open)**
  - **Motor Reverse (Close) = Timer_Done ⋅ NOT(Limit_Close)**

### Stage 3: Actuation
- The PLC digital output (DO) energizes a **relay coil** or **motor driver (e.g., L298N H-bridge)**.
- The H-bridge allows **bidirectional current flow** through the DC motor, enabling forward and reverse rotation.
- A **flyback diode (1N4007)** is placed across the motor terminals to suppress back-EMF.

### Stage 4: Feedback
- Two **limit switches** (LS_OPEN and LS_CLOSE) are mechanically coupled to the door.
- When the door fully opens, LS_OPEN is actuated → PLC sees logic 1 → stops motor, starts **on-delay timer (TON)**.
- After timer expires (typically 3–5 seconds), motor reverses.
- When door fully closes, LS_CLOSE is actuated → motor stops, system returns to idle.

## 2.2 Ladder Logic Truth Table (Core KTU Concept)

Let us define the inputs and outputs:

| Symbol | Address | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I0.0 | SENSOR | Object detected |
| I0.1 | LS_OPEN | Door fully open limit |
| I0.2 | LS_CLOSE | Door fully closed limit |
| Q0.0 | MTR_FWD | Motor runs in OPEN direction |
| Q0.1 | MTR_REV | Motor runs in CLOSE direction |
| T37 | TIMER | ON-delay timer (dwell time) |

**State Transition Truth Table:**

| State | SENSOR | LS_OPEN | LS_CLOSE | TIMER | MTR_FWD | MTR_REV | Action |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 (Idle) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Door closed, motor off |
| 1 (Opening) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Motor forward, door opening |
| 2 (Open Hold) | 1 or 0 | 1 | 0 | counting | 0 | 0 | Motor off, timer running |
| 3 (Closing) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Motor reverse, door closing |
| 4 (Closed) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Return to State 0 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize this table. KTU examiners frequently ask derivations involving these formulas in 7-mark sub-questions.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{d}{t}$ | Linear velocity of door | m/s | Time to traverse door |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity of motor | rad/s | RPM to rad/s |
| 3 | $T_{load} = F \cdot r$ | Torque required at motor shaft | N·m | Motor sizing |
| 4 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Force for door acceleration | N | Newton's 2nd law |
| 5 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power required | W | Motor power rating |
| 6 | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I$ | Electrical input power | W | Power supply sizing |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Gearbox / system efficiency | unitless (0–1) | Gear losses |
| 8 | $T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot G \cdot \eta$ | Output torque after gearbox | N·m | Gear ratio $G$ |
| 9 | $N_{out} = \dfrac{N_{in}}{G}$ | Output speed after gearbox | RPM | Speed reduction |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Kinetic energy of door | J | Impact / safety analysis |
| 11 | $t_{dwell} = \dfrac{PV}{S}$ | Timer preset calc (PLC) | seconds | TON timer preset |
| 12 | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | PLC scan frequency | Hz | Scan time analysis |
| 13 | $\theta = \omega \cdot t$ | Angular displacement | rad | Motor rotation calc |
| 14 | $s = \omega \cdot t$ | Half-step (stepper) | steps | Stepper resolution |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Automatic door systems are foundational in:

- **Building Automation (BAS):** Hospital OT doors, cleanroom doors, bank vault doors.
- **Industrial Safety:** Pneumatic doors in CNC cells that auto-close on operator approach.
- **Automotive:** Power sliding doors in Toyota Vellfire, Hyundai Staria.
- **Public Infrastructure:** Metro/subway platform screen doors (PSDs).
- **PLC Pedagogy:** This is the **most commonly used introductory PLC experiment** worldwide because it cleanly demonstrates I/O mapping, latching, timers, and interlock logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Motor Sizing for a Sliding Door

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Data):**
A sliding door has mass $m = 25$ kg, must travel a distance $d = 0.6$ m in $t = 2$ s, starting from rest and ending at rest. The door slides on rollers with coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.05$. The motor drives the door through a rack-and-pinion of pinion radius $r = 0.025$ m and gear efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Determine:
(a) Required motor torque
(b) Required motor power
(c) Required motor RPM

### Step 1 — Determine Linear Acceleration Profile
Assuming constant acceleration for the first half and constant deceleration for the second half (trapezoidal velocity profile):

$$
t_{accel} = \dfrac{t}{2} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ s}
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{2d}{t^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.6}{(2)^2} = \dfrac{1.2}{4} = 0.3 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Explanation:** The kinematic equation $d = \frac{1}{2}at^2$ applies when the object starts and ends at rest with equal accel/decel phases. Solving for $a$ yields the linear acceleration of the door.

### Step 2 — Determine Maximum Linear Velocity

$$
v_{max} = a \cdot t_{accel} = 0.3 \times 1 = 0.3 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the peak velocity the door reaches at the midpoint of its travel.

### Step 3 — Determine Force at Rack (Linear Force Required)

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g = 0.05 \times 25 \times 9.81 = 12.2625 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{accel} = m \cdot a = 25 \times 0.3 = 7.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{total} = F_{friction} + F_{accel} = 12.2625 + 7.5 = 19.7625 \text{ N}
$$

**Explanation:** The motor must overcome both rolling friction and provide the net force for acceleration. We add them vectorially because they oppose the direction of motion.

### Step 4 — Convert Linear Force to Torque at Pinion

$$
T_{pinion} = F_{total} \cdot r = 19.7625 \times 0.025 = 0.494 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Explanation:** The pinion acts as a lever arm of radius $r$. Multiplying the tangential force by the radius yields the torque at the pinion shaft.

### Step 5 — Account for Gear Efficiency (Motor-Side Torque Required)

$$
T_{motor} = \dfrac{T_{pinion}}{\eta} = \dfrac{0.494}{0.85} = 0.581 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Explanation:** Efficiency losses mean the motor must deliver more torque than the load requires. Dividing by $\eta < 1$ inflates the required motor torque.

### Step 6 — Determine Motor Speed (RPM)

The linear velocity of the door equals the tangential velocity of the pinion:

$$
\omega_{pinion} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{r} = \dfrac{0.3}{0.025} = 12 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Converting to RPM:

$$
N_{pinion} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \omega_{pinion}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{60 \times 12}{2\pi} = \dfrac{720}{6.2832} \approx 114.59 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Explanation:** For a direct drive (no gearbox), the motor runs at pinion speed. With a gearbox of ratio $G$:

$$
N_{motor} = G \cdot N_{pinion} = G \cdot 114.59 \text{ RPM}
$$

For example, if $G = 20$:

$$
N_{motor} = 20 \times 114.59 = 2291.8 \text{ RPM}
$$

### Step 7 — Determine Motor Power

$$
P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor}
$$

Assuming a $20{:}1$ gearbox, $\omega_{motor} = 12 \times 20 = 240$ rad/s:

$$
P_{motor} = 0.581 \times 240 = 139.5 \text{ W}
$$

A standard safety factor of 1.5–2.0 is applied:

$$
P_{rated} = 1.5 \times 139.5 \approx 209 \text{ W} \quad (\text{select a } 250 \text{ W motor})
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PLC Timer Preset Calculation

**Problem:** The door must remain open for a dwell time of $t_{dwell} = 5$ seconds. The PLC TON timer has a time base of $TB = 0.1$ s. Find the **Preset Value (PV)**.

### Step 1 — Recall Timer Preset Formula

$$
PV = \dfrac{t_{dwell}}{TB} = \dfrac{5}{0.1} = 50
$$

**Explanation:** The preset is the number of time-base ticks needed to accumulate the desired delay. The PLC counts internally; when the accumulated value (CV) reaches PV, the timer's output bit turns ON.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ladder Logic Boolean Equations (Sum-of-Products Form)

From the truth table in Section 2.2, the **MTR_FWD** coil must turn ON only in State 1.

**Identify minterms where MTR_FWD = 1:**
- State 1: $SENSOR=1,\ LS\_OPEN=0,\ LS\_CLOSE=1,\ TIMER=0$

Minterm equation:

$$
MTR\_FWD = SENSOR \cdot \overline{LS\_OPEN} \cdot LS\_CLOSE \cdot \overline{TIMER}
$$

For **MTR_REV = 1** in State 3:

$$
MTR\_REV = \overline{SENSOR} \cdot \overline{LS\_OPEN} \cdot \overline{LS\_CLOSE} \cdot TIMER
$$

**Simplified practical form (most KTU answers use this):**

$$
MTR\_FWD = SENSOR \cdot \overline{LS\_OPEN} \cdot \overline{MTR\_REV}
$$

$$
MTR\_REV = TIMER\_DONE \cdot \overline{LS\_CLOSE} \cdot \overline{MTR\_FWD}
$$

**Explanation:** The mutual $\overline{MTR\_REV}$ and $\overline{MTR\_FWD}$ terms form an **electrical interlock** that prevents both relays from being energized simultaneously, which would short-circuit the H-bridge (a common exam pitfall — see Section 5).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Variants

| Sensor Type | Detection Range | Response Time | Cost (INR) | KTU Lab Availability | Suitability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IR Proximity (diffuse)** | 2–80 cm | 5 ms | 80–150 | High | ★★★★ |
| **Ultrasonic HC-SR04** | 2–400 cm | 30 ms (ping cycle) | 100–200 | High | ★★★★★ |
| **PIR (HC-SR501)** | 0–600 cm (human) | 200 ms | 100–180 | Medium | ★★★ (limited to humans) |
| **Inductive Proximity** | 1–8 mm | 1 ms | 150–300 | Low | ★ (metallic only) |
| **Capacitive Proximity** | 1–40 mm | 5 ms | 200–400 | Low | ★★ |
| **Through-beam IR** | up to 10 m | 2 ms | 250–500 | Low | ★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In lab viva, you will be asked **why ultrasonic is preferred over IR** in this experiment. The answer: ultrasonic works independent of target color, transparency, and surface finish, making it robust for varying object types.

## 3.5 Energy and Safety Calculation (Bonus Derivative)

**Closing Impact Energy** (in case limit switch fails):

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (0.3)^2 = 1.125 \text{ J}
$$

**Threshold for human injury:** ~10 J (IEC 60335-2-95 standard for powered doors).

Since $1.125 \text{ J} \ll 10 \text{ J}$, the door is **safe for human contact** at the design speed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

This diagram maps the closed-loop mechatronic interaction between the door, sensors, PLC, and actuator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Person or Object Approaching] -->|Enters detection zone| S1[IR Proximity Sensor]
    S1 -->|Digital HIGH 24V DC| DI1[PLC Digital Input I0.0]
    LSO[Limit Switch OPEN] -->|DI I0.1| PLC1
    LSC[Limit Switch CLOSE] -->|DI I0.2| PLC1
    PLC1[PLC CPU with Ladder Logic Program] -->|DO Q0.0| R1[Relay Coil 1 Forward]
    PLC1 -->|DO Q0.1| R2[Relay Coil 2 Reverse]
    PLC1 -->|Internal flag T37| TIMER[TON On-Delay Timer 5 s]
    R1 -->|Contact closure| HB[H-Bridge Motor Driver L298N]
    R2 -->|Contact closure| HB
    HB -->|Bidirectional DC| M[DC Geared Motor 24V]
    M -->|Torque via rack pinion| DOOR[Sliding Door Leaf]
    DOOR -->|Mechanical coupling| LSO
    DOOR -->|Mechanical coupling| LSC
    TIMER -.->|Triggers reverse| PLC1
```

## 4.2 Ladder Logic Diagram (Textual Representation)

```
    |  SENSOR    LS_OPEN   MTR_REV                              |
    +---[ ]--------[ / ]----[ / ]------------------------( MTR_FWD )---+
    |                                                                    |
    |                                                                    |
    |  TIMER_DONE  LS_CLOSE   MTR_FWD                                   |
    +---[ ]--------[ / ]-------[ / ]----------------------( MTR_REV )---+
    |                                                                    |
    |  MTR_FWD                                                     (T37)|
    +---[ ]------------------------------------------------------(TON) |
    |   preset = 50 (5 s)                                              |
    |                                                                    |
    |  MTR_FWD                                                        |
    +---[ ]-----------------------------------------------------( LS_OPEN_LATCH )-+
    |  MTR_REV                                                        |
    +---[ ]----------------------------------------------------( LS_CLOSE_LATCH )-+
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Ladder:** Each horizontal line is a "rung." A coil (output) on the right is energized only if **all** the contacts in series on its left are TRUE (closed). `[ ]` is a Normally Open contact (NO), `[ / ]` is Normally Closed (NC). An NC contact passes current when its associated bit is 0.

## 4.3 State Transition Flowchart (Process Flow)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Opening: SENSOR = 1 AND LS_CLOSED = 1
    Opening --> OpenHold: LS_OPEN = 1 (motor stops)
    OpenHold --> Closing: Timer Done (5 s elapsed)
    Closing --> Idle: LS_CLOSE = 1 (motor stops)
    Opening --> EmergencyStop: E_STOP pressed
    Closing --> EmergencyStop: E_STOP pressed
    EmergencyStop --> Idle: E_STOP released AND LS_CLOSED = 1
```

## 4.4 Hardware Wiring Schematic (Pin Map)

| PLC Terminal | Connected Device | Wire Color (KTU Lab Convention) | Voltage Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I0.0 | IR Sensor OUT | Red | 24 V DC |
| I0.1 | LS_OPEN (COM) | Yellow | 24 V DC |
| I0.2 | LS_CLOSE (COM) | Green | 24 V DC |
| Q0.0 | Relay K1 (Forward) Coil | Blue | 24 V DC |
| Q0.1 | Relay K2 (Reverse) Coil | White | 24 V DC |
| 24V+ | Sensor Vcc, Relay Vcc | Red | 24 V DC |
| 24V- (0V) | All commons | Black | 0 V |
| L / N | 230 V AC mains to PLC PSU | Brown / Blue | 230 V AC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Callout:** Never connect 230 V AC and 24 V DC wires in the same cable duct. KTU lab safety audit will fail your record if so.

## 4.5 H-Bridge Motor Direction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[PLC Q0.0 HIGH] -->|Energizes| B[Relay K1 Closes]
    B -->|Current flows A to B| C[Motor Terminal A Positive]
    C --> D[Motor Spins Forward OPEN]
    A2[PLC Q0.1 HIGH] -->|Energizes| B2[Relay K2 Closes]
    B2 -->|Current flows B to A| C2[Motor Terminal B Positive]
    C2 --> D2[Motor Spins Reverse CLOSE]
    A3[Both Q0.0 and Q0.1 HIGH] -->|INTERLOCK FAULT| E3[Short Circuit H-Bridge Damaged]
    E3 -.->|PREVENTED BY| F3[Mutual NC Contacts in Ladder]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define a limit switch and state its role in an automatic door system."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **limit switch** is an electromechanical sensor that detects the presence or absence of an object, or the attainment of a predefined mechanical position, by means of physical contact with a lever, plunger, or roller actuator. It provides a discrete (binary) electrical output: contacts are either **Normally Open (NO)** or **Normally Closed (NC)**.

**Role in automatic door system (2 marks):**
In the automatic door experiment, two limit switches (LS_OPEN and LS_CLOSE) are mechanically coupled to the door leaf. When the door reaches its fully open position, LS_OPEN is mechanically actuated, sending a logic HIGH signal to the PLC input. The PLC then de-energizes the forward motor coil and starts the on-delay timer. Similarly, LS_CLOSE signals the end of the closing stroke. This forms the **positional feedback loop** essential for safe operation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"What is the purpose of an H-bridge in a DC motor driving circuit? Why is it needed for an automatic door?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **H-bridge** is an electronic circuit (using 4 switching elements — typically transistors or relays arranged in an 'H' configuration) that allows a DC motor to be driven in **both forward and reverse directions** from a single polarity power supply.

**Why needed for automatic door (2 marks):**
The automatic door requires the motor to rotate **clockwise to open** and **counter-clockwise to close**. A single-ended driver (e.g., simple transistor switch) can only rotate the motor in one direction. The H-bridge, controlled by two PLC outputs (Q0.0 and Q0.1), reverses the polarity across the motor terminals, enabling bidirectional motion. **Electrical interlocks** in the ladder logic ensure both directions are never activated simultaneously, preventing a short circuit.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often forget to draw the **NC interlock contacts** in the ladder diagram → **lose 2 marks**.
> - Confusing **NO (Normally Open)** and **NC (Normally Closed)** contact symbols in ladder logic → **lose 1–2 marks**.
> - Forgetting to specify the **timer time base and preset value** when discussing the dwell time → **lose 1 mark**.
> - Writing the ladder diagram in computer logic (AND/OR gates) instead of **IEC 61131-3 ladder notation** → **lose 2 marks**.
> - Not labeling input/output addresses (I0.0, Q0.0, etc.) on the diagram → **lose 1 mark**.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the components and working of an **automatic door opening and closing system** using a PLC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop the **ladder logic program** for the above system. Explain the function of each rung. Assume a dwell time of 4 seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The automatic door system consists of the following major components, organized into four functional blocks:

**1. Input / Sensing Block:**
- One **IR Proximity Sensor** (or ultrasonic sensor) mounted on the approach side. Output: 24 V DC when object detected.
- Two **Limit Switches**: LS_OPEN and LS_CLOSE, mechanically linked to the door.

**2. Control Block (PLC):**
- A **Programmable Logic Controller** (e.g., Siemens LOGO!, Delta DVP-14SS, or Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1000).
- Powered by 24 V DC SMPS.
- Ladder logic program stored in non-volatile memory.

**3. Output / Actuation Block:**
- Two **24 V DC relays** (K1 for forward, K2 for reverse) or an **L298N H-bridge driver**.
- One **24 V DC geared motor** (30–100 RPM) coupled to the door via rack-and-pinion or lead screw.

**4. Mechanical Load Block:**
- The door leaf (sliding or swing type) with rollers and tracks.

**Working Principle:**
At power-on, the door is at the closed position, with LS_CLOSE actuated (logic 1). When a person approaches, the IR sensor output goes HIGH. The PLC detects this at input I0.0. Since LS_OPEN is not yet actuated (logic 0), rung 1 of the ladder is TRUE → Q0.0 (MTR_FWD) is energized → relay K1 closes → motor runs forward → door slides open. When the door fully opens, LS_OPEN is actuated → input I0.1 becomes 1 → rung 1 breaks → Q0.0 de-energizes. Simultaneously, rung 3 starts the on-delay timer T37. After 4 seconds (preset = 40 at 0.1 s time base), T37's output enables rung 2 → Q0.1 (MTR_REV) energizes → motor reverses → door closes. When LS_CLOSE actuates, the motor stops, and the system returns to the idle state.

**Block Diagram (Verbal):**
Person → Sensor → PLC Input → [CPU + Ladder] → PLC Output → Relay → H-Bridge → Motor → Door → Limit Switches → PLC Input (loop closed)

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Ladder Logic Program (4-Rung Solution):**

**Rung 1 — Open Direction (Motor Forward):**
```
| I0.0(SENSOR)  I0.1(LS_OPEN)  Q0.1(MTR_REV) |  Q0.0(MTR_FWD)
+----[ ]----------[ / ]-----------[ / ]--------+---( )---+
```
**Explanation:** Q0.0 turns ON only when sensor detects an object (I0.0 = 1), door is not yet fully open (LS_OPEN = 0, so NC contact closed), and reverse relay is not active (interlock). This drives the motor in the open direction.

**Rung 2 — Close Direction (Motor Reverse):**
```
| T37(TIMER)   I0.2(LS_CLOSE)  Q0.0(MTR_FWD) |  Q0.1(MTR_REV)
+----[ ]----------[ / ]------------[ / ]--------+---( )---+
```
**Explanation:** After 4 seconds, T37 turns ON. If door is not yet fully closed (LS_CLOSE = 0) and motor is not in forward direction (interlock), Q0.1 energizes → door closes.

**Rung 3 — On-Delay Timer (Dwell Time):**
```
| Q0.0(MTR_FWD) |
+----[ ]---------+---[TON T37, PT = 40]---+
```
**Explanation:** The timer starts the moment the door begins opening. Preset = 40 ticks × 0.1 s/tick = 4 seconds. When elapsed, T37 DONE bit enables Rung 2.

**Rung 4 — Seal-In (Latching) for Continuous Run:**
```
| I0.0(SENSOR)  Q0.0(MTR_FWD) |  Q0.0(MTR_FWD) Internal
+----[ ]----------[ ]----------+---( )---+
```
**Explanation:** This is a **seal-in (latching) contact** that keeps Q0.0 energized even if the sensor momentarily loses the object (e.g., a tall person passes quickly). The contact Q0.0 in parallel with I0.0 holds the motor on until LS_OPEN breaks the circuit.

**Function Summary Table:**

| Rung | Function |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Energize forward motor when object detected and door not open |
| 2 | Energize reverse motor after timer expires and door not closed |
| 3 | Start 4-second dwell timer when door starts opening |
| 4 | Latch the open command to handle momentary sensor dropouts |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **interfacing of an IR sensor and a DC motor** with a PLC for an automatic door application. Draw the wiring diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sliding door of mass 30 kg is to be opened in 3 seconds over a distance of 0.9 m, starting from rest. The pinion radius is 0.03 m, gear efficiency is 0.8, and coefficient of friction is 0.04. Determine: **(i)** the required motor torque, **(ii)** motor RPM (no gearbox), and **(iii)** motor power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**IR Sensor Interfacing:**
- The IR proximity sensor has 3 terminals: **Vcc (+5 V or +24 V)**, **GND**, and **OUT**.
- Vcc is connected to the PLC's sensor power rail (+24 V).
- GND is connected to the PLC's 0 V common terminal.
- The OUT terminal is wired to **PLC input terminal I0.0**.
- A **pull-down resistor (10 kΩ)** is sometimes used if the sensor has an open-collector output.
- The sensor's LED indicator (if present) shows detection status; a logic HIGH at I0.0 indicates an object is present.

**DC Motor Interfacing (with H-Bridge):**
- A single DC motor cannot be driven directly by a PLC output (current limitation ~0.5 A).
- Therefore, an **H-bridge driver IC (L298N)** or **relay-based H-bridge** is used as an intermediary.
- The L298N has input pins IN1, IN2, ENA (enable), and output pins OUT1, OUT2.
- IN1 is connected to PLC output Q0.0 (forward command).
- IN2 is connected to PLC output Q0.1 (reverse command).
- ENA is tied to +5 V (always enabled) or driven by a PWM output for speed control.
- OUT1 and OUT2 connect to the two motor terminals.
- The motor's power is supplied from an external **24 V DC, 2 A SMPS** (separate from the PLC's logic supply to avoid noise).
- A **flyback diode (1N4007)** across each motor terminal protects against back-EMF.

**Wiring Diagram (Verbal Schematic):**
```
   +24V ----[IR Sensor Vcc]
   +24V ----[PLC Sensor Power]
   GND  ----[IR Sensor GND]----[PLC 0V common]
   IR.OUT ----[PLC I0.0]
   PLC Q0.0 ----[L298N IN1]
   PLC Q0.1 ----[L298N IN2]
   L298N ENA ----[+5V (always on)]
   L298N OUT1 ----[Motor Terminal A]
   L298N OUT2 ----[Motor Terminal B]
   +24V (motor) ----[L298N VS]
   GND (motor) ----[L298N GND]
   Limit Switch LS_OPEN ----[PLC I0.1]
   Limit Switch LS_CLOSE ----[PLC I0.2]
```

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m = 30$ kg
- Distance $d = 0.9$ m
- Time $t = 3$ s
- Pinion radius $r = 0.03$ m
- Gear efficiency $\eta = 0.8$
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.04$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Calculate acceleration (trapezoidal profile):**

$$
t_{accel} = \dfrac{t}{2} = 1.5 \text{ s}
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{2d}{t^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.9}{9} = 0.2 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate total linear force:**

$$
F_{friction} = \mu m g = 0.04 \times 30 \times 9.81 = 11.772 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{accel} = m a = 30 \times 0.2 = 6 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 11.772 + 6 = 17.772 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate torque at pinion (output of motor):**

$$
T_{pinion} = F_{total} \cdot r = 17.772 \times 0.03 = 0.5332 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Account for efficiency:**

$$
T_{motor} = \dfrac{T_{pinion}}{\eta} = \dfrac{0.5332}{0.8} = 0.6665 \text{ N·m} \approx 0.667 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate maximum linear velocity:**

$$
v_{max} = a \cdot t_{accel} = 0.2 \times 1.5 = 0.3 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate angular velocity at pinion:**

$$
\omega_{pinion} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{r} = \dfrac{0.3}{0.03} = 10 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 7: Convert to RPM (no gearbox, so motor RPM = pinion RPM):**

$$
N_{motor} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \omega_{pinion}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{60 \times 10}{2\pi} = \dfrac{600}{6.2832} \approx 95.49 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 8: Calculate motor power:**

$$
P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{pinion} = 0.6665 \times 10 = 6.665 \text{ W}
$$

Applying safety factor of 2:

$$
P_{rated} = 2 \times 6.665 \approx 13.33 \text{ W} \quad (\text{select a } 15 \text{ W or 20 W DC motor})
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T_{motor} = 0.667$ N·m
- (ii) $N_{motor} \approx 95.5$ RPM
- (iii) $P_{motor} = 6.67$ W (select ~15–20 W motor with safety factor)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

### Core Concept Bullets

- ✅ The automatic door is a **Sense → Decide → Actuate → Feedback** mechatronic system with **discrete event control**.
- ✅ Components: **Sensor (IR / Ultrasonic) → PLC → Relay / H-Bridge → DC Motor → Door → Limit Switches** (back to PLC).
- ✅ Two limit switches are mandatory: **LS_OPEN** (stops forward motion) and **LS_CLOSE** (stops reverse motion).
- ✅ The **dwell timer (TON)** keeps the door open for a preset period (typically 3–5 seconds).
- ✅ **Interlock contacts** (NC of MTR_FWD in series with MTR_REV rung, and vice versa) prevent simultaneous forward and reverse commands.
- ✅ **Seal-in (latching) contact** holds the motor ON even if the sensor input drops momentarily.
- ✅ The H-bridge (L298N) provides **bidirectional motor control** using two PLC digital outputs.
- ✅ PLC scan time (~10–20 ms) must be **slower than sensor response time** to avoid missing pulses.
- ✅ Flyback diodes (1N4007) across motor terminals suppress **back-EMF** that can damage PLC outputs.
- ✅ A **pull-down resistor (10 kΩ)** is recommended for open-collector sensor outputs.

### Exam Power-Phrases (Use These for Full Marks)

- *"The system employs **discrete event control (DEC)** with **positional feedback** through electromechanical limit switches."*
- *"The PLC executes the ladder logic in a **cyclic scan** manner: read inputs, execute program, update outputs."*
- *"**Electrical interlocking** via normally-closed contacts ensures the H-bridge is never short-circuited."*
- *"The TON timer's **preset value** is calculated as PV = $t_{dwell}$ / TimeBase."*
- *"The motor's **rated torque** is selected with a safety factor of 1.5–2.0 to account for start-up transients and wear."*

### Common Numerical Values to Memorize

- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Standard timer time base = **0.1 s** (Siemens S7-200) or **0.01 s** (Allen-Bradley)
- Standard IR sensor range in lab: **30 cm**
- Standard ultrasonic range: **2 cm to 400 cm**
- Standard PLC digital input voltage: **24 V DC**
- Standard relay coil current: **30–70 mA**

### Viva-Voce Favorites (Prepare These!)

1. **"Why use a PLC instead of a microcontroller?"** → PLC is **industrial-grade**, has **built-in I/O isolation**, **noise immunity**, **ladder logic** readable by electricians, and **no programming of firmware** required.
2. **"What happens if both limit switches fail?"** → The motor would overrun; the door would slam into the wall. **Safety mitigation:** add **mechanical end-stops** and **overcurrent protection** in the H-bridge.
3. **"Why not use a stepper motor?"** → Stepper is **open-loop**; if the door jams, the stepper loses steps silently. DC motor with **limit switch feedback** is **closed-loop** and **fail-safe**.
4. **"Can we use a pneumatic actuator instead?"** → Yes, especially for **heavier doors**. Pneumatic gives **higher force** and **inherent compliance** (cushioning).
5. **"What is the role of the timer?"** → Ensures the door does not close on a slow-moving person; it provides a **safety dwell period** after entry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data acquisition for virtual instrumentation
topics.content:
