> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRL607 - MECHATRONICS SYSTEMS & PLC LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Data acquisition for virtual instrumentation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the systematic process of sampling real-world physical phenomena (such as temperature, pressure, force, displacement, flow, voltage, or current) and converting them into a digital format that a computer can process, store, analyze, and visualize. **Virtual Instrumentation (VI)** is a paradigm in measurement and automation where the traditional hardware-based instruments (oscilloscope, multimeter, function generator, spectrum analyzer) are replaced by a **general-purpose computer equipped with DAQ hardware and flexible software** (e.g., **LabVIEW**, MATLAB, or Python). The "instrument" exists virtually inside the computer as a graphical front-panel constructed by the developer, while the actual signal measurement is performed by the DAQ card. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRL607 - Module 2):** Students must understand the **DAQ hardware chain**, signal conditioning, ADC/DAC operations, sampling theory, and the implementation of these concepts using LabVIEW or equivalent virtual instrumentation software for a complete mechatronics experiment. The formal block representation of a DAQ system is: $$\text{Physical Signal} \rightarrow \text{Sensor/Transducer} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Computer (Software)}$$ ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are a foreigner in Kerala and you cannot read Malayalam. To understand a bus destination board, you ask a local **translator** to read it aloud to you in English. - The **bus board** = the physical signal in the real world (temperature, vibration, voltage). - The **local person** = the **sensor/transducer** (converts physical quantity to electrical signal). - **Interpreting and translating** = **Signal Conditioning + ADC** (cleaning up and converting the signal into computer-friendly digital numbers). - **You (the listener with a notebook)** = the **computer with software** (LabVIEW), which interprets, displays, and records the data. Just as you could ask the translator to speak louder, filter out street noise, or summarize key points — the DAQ system lets you "listen" to the physical world with adjustable precision, speed, and filtering. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics The performance of any DAQ system is evaluated using these universal metrics: - **Sampling Rate (fs)** — typically expressed in **Samples per Second (S/s)** or **Hz**. - **Resolution (n)** — measured in **bits** (typically **8, 12, 14, 16, or 24 bits**). - **Input Range** — expressed in **Volts (V)**. - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — measured in **decibels (dB)**. - **Bandwidth** — expressed in **Hertz (Hz)**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Transducer vs. Sensor:** A **sensor** detects a physical quantity, while a **transducer** converts one form of energy into another. In modern usage, the terms are used interchangeably. For example, a thermocouple is a **temperature sensor** that acts as a **transducer** by converting thermal energy into millivolts of EMF. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown A complete Data Acquisition System for virtual instrumentation is organized into a precise chain of stages. Each stage has a clearly defined engineering role. ### Stage 1 — Sensing (Transduction) - The physical phenomenon (force, light, pH, acceleration) is sensed by a **transducer**. - Output is typically a low-level analog electrical signal: microvolts ($\mu$V), millivolts (mV), or milliamps (mA). - Examples: Thermocouple (mV/°C), RTD ($\Omega$ vs. temperature), LVDT (V/mm), Piezoelectric sensor (pC/N). ### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning Raw sensor output is rarely usable directly by an ADC. Signal conditioning prepares it: - **Amplification:** Boosts low-amplitude signals (e.g., from mV to V) using instrumentation amplifiers. - **Filtering:** Removes high-frequency noise using low-pass filters (anti-aliasing filter is mandatory before ADC). - **Isolation:** Protects the computer from voltage surges using opto-isolators. - **Linearization:** Corrects non-linear sensor responses (e.g., thermistor curve fitting). - **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Required for accurate thermocouple readings. ### Stage 3 — Multiplexing - A single ADC is shared among many input channels by an **analog multiplexer (MUX)**. - Channels are scanned sequentially (round-robin) or simultaneously (using sample-and-hold arrays). ### Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) - The conditioned analog signal is sampled and quantized into a digital binary word. - Common ADC architectures in DAQ: **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)**, **Delta-Sigma ($\Delta$-$\Sigma$)**, and **Flash ADC**. ### Stage 5 — Computer Interface and Software - Digital data is transferred to the host computer via **PCI, PCIe, USB, Ethernet, or PXI**. - Software (LabVIEW VIs, MATLAB scripts, Python) processes, displays, logs, and analyzes data. - The graphical front-panel (knobs, graphs, indicators) constitutes the **"virtual instrument"**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Engineering Meaning** | |---------------|-------------|-----------|--------------------------| | Number of discrete levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless | Levels available with $n$-bit ADC | | Quantization Step (LSB size) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) | Smallest detectable voltage step | | Nyquist Sampling Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling to avoid aliasing | | Quantization Error (max) | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts (V) | Inherent rounding error of ADC | | Quantization Noise (RMS) | $e_{rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Volts (V) | RMS noise voltage | | Theoretical SNR | $\text{SNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | Signal-to-noise ratio of an ideal $n$-bit ADC | | Throughput | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds/sample | Time per single sample | | Multi-channel Scan Time | $T_{scan} = N_{ch} \cdot T_{sample} + T_{settling}$ | seconds | Time to scan one full multiplexer cycle | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Practice:** In KTU board examinations, students are often asked to compute **ADC resolution (LSB)**, **number of levels**, or **minimum sampling rate**. Memorizing the formulas above will directly secure full marks in such numericals. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility Virtual instrumentation DAQ systems are deployed in: - **Automotive:** Engine control unit (ECU) testing, vibration analysis of chassis. - **Biomedical:** ECG, EEG, EMG signal acquisition — instruments like the **Biopac MP36** are built on this exact principle. - **Industrial Automation:** SCADA systems, PLC-based process control, robotics. - **Structural Health Monitoring:** Bridge vibration monitoring using NI CompactDAQ. - **Aerospace:** Flight data recorders ("black box"), wind tunnel instrumentation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Step Size ($\Delta$) and Number of Levels ($L$) ### Given - An ADC with $n$ bits of resolution. - Full-scale input voltage range $V_{FS}$ (peak-to-peak input range of the ADC). ### Step 1 — Identify the total count of discrete codes For an $n$-bit ADC, every bit contributes one of two states ($0$ or $1$). The total number of distinct binary codes is therefore: $$L = 2^n$$ For an 8-bit ADC: $L = 2^8 = 256$ levels. For a 12-bit ADC: $L = 2^{12} = 4096$ levels. For a 16-bit ADC: $L = 2^{16} = 65536$ levels. ### Step 2 — Define the size of one quantum (Least Significant Bit) The full-scale voltage is divided uniformly among all $L$ levels. The width of a single quantum — also called the **LSB size** or **resolution in volts** — is: $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{L} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$ This means a 12-bit ADC with a $0$ to $10$ V input range gives: $$\Delta = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.4414 \text{ mV}$$ So the smallest voltage change the ADC can distinguish is **2.44 mV**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Quantization Error (Maximum and RMS) ### Step 1 — Maximum Quantization Error When an analog signal falls between two quantization levels, the ADC rounds it to the nearest level. The maximum possible difference between the true analog value and the quantized digital value is half the width of one quantum: $$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2}$$ The sign ($\pm$) simply indicates the rounding can go either way. ### Step 2 — RMS Quantization Noise Assuming the quantization error is uniformly distributed between $-\Delta/2$ and $+\Delta/2$, the RMS (Root Mean Square) value is obtained by integrating the square of the error over its probability density: $$\overline{e^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \, de$$ Computing the integral step by step: $$\overline{e^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$ $$\overline{e^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left( \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 (\Delta/2)^3}{3}$$ $$\overline{e^2} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ Taking the square root gives the RMS quantization noise: $$e_{rms} = \sqrt{\overline{e^2}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) ### Step 1 — Express signal power For a full-scale sine wave of amplitude $A$, the RMS signal value is $A/\sqrt{2}$, and the signal power is: $$P_{signal} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$ The full-scale peak-to-peak amplitude spans $2^n \cdot \Delta$ codes, so $A = 2^{n-1} \cdot \Delta$. Substituting: $$P_{signal} = \frac{(2^{n-1} \cdot \Delta)^2}{2} = \frac{2^{2n-2} \cdot \Delta^2}{2} = 2^{2n-3} \cdot \Delta^2$$ ### Step 2 — Express quantization noise power From the previous derivation: $$P_{noise} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute SNR as a ratio $$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{2^{2n-3} \cdot \Delta^2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2n-3} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to decibels $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 n \log_{10}(2)$$ Using $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$: $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Each additional bit of ADC resolution contributes approximately **6 dB** of dynamic range. Doubling the sampling rate does **NOT** improve SNR — it only increases bandwidth (the highest frequency you can capture without aliasing). ## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) **Problem:** A 12-bit DAQ card has an input range of $-10$ V to $+10$ V. A thermocouple outputs a voltage of $32.768$ mV. Compute: (i) Quantization step size, (ii) Maximum quantization error, (iii) Digital output code. **Solution:** Step (i) — Full-scale range: $$V_{FS} = (+10) - (-10) = 20 \text{ V}$$ $$\Delta = \frac{20}{2^{12}} = \frac{20}{4096} = 4.8828 \text{ mV}$$ Step (ii) — Maximum error: $$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{4.8828}{2} = \pm 2.4414 \text{ mV}$$ Step (iii) — Digital code (linear mapping with zero at $0$ V): $$\text{Code} = \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta} + 2^{n-1} = \frac{32.768 \text{ mV}}{4.8828 \text{ mV}} + 2048 \approx 6.71 + 2048 \approx 2055$$ $$\boxed{\text{Code} = 2055 \;\; (\text{decimal}) \;\; \approx \;\; 0\text{x}807 \;\; (\text{hex})}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Sampling Rate | **Signal Type** | **Typical Bandwidth** | **Recommended $f_s$ (Nyquist)** | **Practical $f_s$ (5–10x)** | |------------------|------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------| | Temperature (slow) | 0–1 Hz | $\geq$ 2 Hz | 10 Hz | | ECG (heart) | 0.05–150 Hz | $\geq$ 300 Hz | 500–1000 Hz | | Audio (voice) | 300 Hz–3.4 kHz | $\geq$ 6.8 kHz | 8–44.1 kHz | | Vibration (machine) | 10 Hz–10 kHz | $\geq$ 20 kHz | 50–100 kHz | | Ultrasonic | 20–200 kHz | $\geq$ 400 kHz | 1–5 MHz | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete DAQ System for Virtual Instrumentation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Quantity
Temperature, Pressure, Force] --> B[Sensor / Transducer
Thermocouple, RTD, LVDT] B --> C[Signal Conditioning
Amplify, Filter, Isolate, Linearize] C --> D[Analog Multiplexer
MUX] D --> E[Sample and Hold
S and H] E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter
ADC 12 to 16 bit] F --> G[Computer Interface
USB, PCI, Ethernet] G --> H[Host Computer
LabVIEW or MATLAB] H --> I[Virtual Front Panel
Graphs, Indicators, Knobs] H --> J[Data Storage and Analysis
CSV, Database, FFT] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Anti-Aliasing Filter Placement ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Analog Signal
f_max = 500 Hz] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Low Pass Filter
fc = 600 Hz, 4th Order Butterworth] B --> C[Sampler at f_s = 2 kHz
Satisfies Nyquist f_s ge 2 f_max] C --> D[Quantizer and ADC
12 bit, 0 to 10 V] D --> E[Digital Data Stream
Binary Codes] E --> F[Reconstruction
DAC and Smoothing Filter] F --> G[Reconstructed Analog Signal
Low Pass Filtered] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Software Architecture of a LabVIEW Virtual Instrument ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FrontPanel["FRONT PANEL"] P1[Waveform Graph Indicator] P2[Knob for Sampling Rate] P3[Numeric Indicator for Temperature] P4[Boolean Stop Button] end subgraph BlockDiagram["BLOCK DIAGRAM"] D1[DAQ Assistant VI] D2[Filter Express VI] D3[Statistical Analysis VI] D4[While Loop Structure] D5[File Write to TDMS or CSV] end P2 -- Wire Control --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> D5 D3 -- Wire Data --> P1 D3 -- Wire Data --> P3 P4 -- Wire Boolean --> D4 D4 --> D1 D4 --> D5 ``` ## 4.4 Signal Conversion Process (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Continuous Time
Analog Signal] --> B[Sampling
Impulse Train Modulation] B --> C[Sampled Signal
Discrete Time Continuous Amplitude] C --> D[Quantization
Round to Nearest Level] D --> E[Digital Signal
Discrete Time and Discrete Amplitude] ``` ## 4.5 Signal Conditioning Subsystems ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Sensor Output
mV Level] --> B[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 100 to 1000] B --> C[Low Pass Anti Alias Filter
fc at f_s divided by 2.5] C --> D[Opto Isolator
Galvanic Isolation 1500 V] D --> E[Level Shifter
Match ADC Input Range] E --> F[ADC Input Pin
0 to 10 V or plus minus 10 V] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **Define Data Acquisition System. List any four components of a DAQ system.** **Model Answer:** A **Data Acquisition System (DAQ)** is a measurement system that samples real-world physical signals (such as temperature, pressure, or voltage), converts them into a digital format using an ADC, and processes them on a computer for analysis, display, and storage. **Four components of a DAQ system:** 1. **Sensor / Transducer** — converts physical quantity into electrical signal. 2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — amplifies, filters, and isolates the raw signal. 3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts the conditioned analog signal into digital codes. 4. **Host Computer with Software** (e.g., LabVIEW) — processes, displays, and stores the digital data. *(Optionally also accepted: Multiplexer, Sample-and-Hold circuit, Anti-aliasing filter.)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Differentiate between virtual instrumentation and traditional instrumentation.** **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Traditional Instrumentation** | **Virtual Instrumentation** | |------------|---------------------------------|------------------------------| | Implementation | Hardware-defined (op-amps, ASICs) | Software-defined on a PC | | Flexibility | Fixed functionality | User-programmable, reconfigurable | | Cost | High for multiple instruments | Low — software defines new instruments | | Display | Physical knobs, dials, LCD | Graphical front-panel on screen | | Upgradation | Replace hardware | Update or modify the VI code | | Data Storage | External paper/loggers | Direct digital logging to disk | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often confuse **resolution in bits** with **quantization step in volts**. Always compute both and state units explicitly. > - In Nyquist-related questions, forgetting to multiply by **2** to get the minimum sampling rate is the most common error (costs **3 marks**). > - In ADC code-conversion questions, students forget to add the **offset** $2^{n-1}$ for bipolar input ranges. This single mistake can lose **2 to 3 marks**. > - When drawing DAQ block diagrams, **signal conditioning** must always be shown **before** the multiplexer and ADC — examiners deduct marks for incorrect ordering. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2 Model]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a PC-based Data Acquisition System for virtual instrumentation. Discuss the role of signal conditioning and anti-aliasing filter in detail. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A PC-based DAQ system consists of the following sequential blocks: 1. **Sensor / Transducer:** Converts the physical quantity (e.g., temperature, strain, pressure) into a proportional electrical signal. Example: K-type thermocouple produces ~41 $\mu$V/°C. 2. **Signal Conditioning:** The raw signal is at very low amplitude and contaminated with noise. Conditioning performs: - **Amplification** using an instrumentation amplifier (INA128, AD620) to bring the signal to the ADC input range. - **Filtering** — typically a 2nd or 4th order Butterworth **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** with cutoff $f_c = f_s / 2.5$. - **Isolation** through opto-couplers or transformers (1.5 kV or higher) to protect the computer. - **Cold-junction compensation** (for thermocouples) and **linearization** (for RTDs and thermistors). 3. **Multiplexer (MUX):** Time-shares one ADC across multiple input channels, switching sequentially. 4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Holds the analog voltage constant during ADC conversion to prevent aperture error. 5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter:** Samples and quantizes the held voltage into a binary code. Resolution typically 12, 14, 16, or 24 bits. 6. **Computer Interface:** Data is transferred to the PC via USB, PCI, PCIe, or Ethernet. 7. **Software Layer:** LabVIEW/MATLAB/Python processes, displays, and logs the data on a virtual front-panel. **Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter:** According to the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, any analog signal containing frequency components up to $f_{max}$ must be sampled at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Any frequency content above $f_s/2$ will "fold back" into the baseband and appear as a false low-frequency component — this is **aliasing**. To prevent this, an **analog anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $f_c$ slightly below $f_s/2$ is placed **before** the ADC. A practical choice is a 4th-order Butterworth filter with $f_c = 0.4 \cdot f_s$. Without this filter, high-frequency noise or interference would corrupt the digitized data irreversibly. **(b)** A 16-bit ADC has an input range of $-5$ V to $+5$ V. Compute: **(i)** the number of quantization levels, **(ii)** the size of one LSB, **(iii)** the maximum quantization error, **(iv)** the theoretical SNR in dB. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $n = 16$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V (since $-5$ to $+5$ V spans 10 V). **(i) Number of quantization levels:** $$L = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ levels}$$ **(ii) Size of one LSB (quantization step):** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{65536} = 152.588 \text{ }\mu\text{V}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta \approx 152.59 \text{ }\mu\text{V}}$$ **(iii) Maximum quantization error:** $$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 76.294 \text{ }\mu\text{V}$$ $$\boxed{e_{q,\max} \approx \pm 76.29 \text{ }\mu\text{V}}$$ **(iv) Theoretical SNR:** $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 16 + 1.76$$ $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$ $$\boxed{\text{SNR} \approx 98.08 \text{ dB}}$$ **Conclusion:** Each additional bit adds approximately 6 dB of dynamic range, justifying the use of 16-bit or 24-bit ADCs in precision instrumentation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Model]` **(a)** Explain the concept of virtual instrumentation with the help of a LabVIEW front-panel and block diagram example. List any **three advantages** of VI over conventional instruments. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept of Virtual Instrumentation:** Virtual Instrumentation (VI) is the technique of using a **general-purpose PC** equipped with DAQ hardware and graphical programming software (like LabVIEW) to emulate the functionality of traditional standalone instruments such as oscilloscopes, function generators, and spectrum analyzers. The "instrument" is defined by software rather than built from dedicated hardware circuits. **Example — Temperature Monitoring VI in LabVIEW:** - **Front Panel (User Interface):** Contains a waveform chart to plot temperature vs. time, a numeric indicator for the current temperature value, a knob to set the sampling rate, and a Boolean STOP button. - **Block Diagram (Source Code):** Contains a **DAQ Assistant VI** that reads voltage from the DAQ card, a **Filter Express VI** to remove 50 Hz mains noise, a **Thermocouple Linearization VI**, and a **While Loop** to continuously acquire and display data until STOP is pressed. The block diagram wires connect controls to indicators, defining data flow. The same hardware (DAQ card + thermocouple) can be reused to build a different "instrument" simply by rewriting the LabVIEW code. **Three Advantages of VI:** 1. **Flexibility & Reconfigurability:** A single DAQ system can perform the role of multiple instruments (oscilloscope + spectrum analyzer + data logger) by changing software. 2. **Lower Cost & Footprint:** Eliminates the need to purchase many separate instruments; reduces hardware cost and bench space. 3. **Powerful Data Processing:** Built-in functions for FFT, filtering, statistical analysis, and direct export to databases/cloud — capabilities often absent in standalone instruments. **(b)** An audio signal contains frequency components up to **20 kHz**. The signal is sampled by a 14-bit ADC with a 0–5 V input range. Determine: **(i)** the minimum sampling rate (Nyquist rate), **(ii)** the LSB size in mV, **(iii)** the RMS quantization noise voltage, **(iv)** the theoretical SNR. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $f_{max} = 20$ kHz, $n = 14$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V. **(i) Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist rate):** $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 20 \text{ kHz} = 40 \text{ kHz}$$ In practice, audio systems use $f_s = 44.1$ kHz (CD quality) or 48 kHz. So: $$\boxed{f_{s,\min} = 40 \text{ kHz}}$$ **(ii) LSB size:** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{14}} = \frac{5}{16384} = 305.176 \text{ }\mu\text{V}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta \approx 0.3052 \text{ mV}}$$ **(iii) RMS quantization noise:** $$e_{rms} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{0.3052 \text{ mV}}{3.4641}$$ $$\boxed{e_{rms} \approx 88.1 \text{ }\mu\text{V}}$$ **(iv) Theoretical SNR:** $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}$$ $$\boxed{\text{SNR} \approx 86.04 \text{ dB}}$$ This SNR corresponds roughly to 14 bits of effective resolution — sufficient for telephony and consumer audio. Professional studio audio uses 24-bit ADCs to achieve ~146 dB SNR. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Acquisition for Virtual Instrumentation** - ☐ **DAQ Chain (Memorize in Order):** Sensor → Signal Conditioning → Multiplexer → Sample & Hold → ADC → Computer Interface → Software Display. - ☐ **Signal Conditioning ALWAYS precedes ADC** — never reverse this in diagrams. - ☐ **Anti-aliasing filter** is a low-pass filter with $f_c \approx f_s/2.5$ placed **before** the sampler. - ☐ **Number of levels** $L = 2^n$; **LSB size** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$; **Max error** $\pm \Delta/2$; **RMS noise** $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$. - ☐ **Theoretical SNR (dB)** $= 6.02n + 1.76$ — every extra bit adds **6 dB** of dynamic range. - ☐ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; practical $f_s$ is 5–10x $f_{max}$ to leave guard band. - ☐ **Virtual Instrumentation** = Software-defined instrument on a PC using LabVIEW/MATLAB with DAQ hardware. - ☐ **VI advantages:** Flexibility, low cost, advanced analysis (FFT, filtering, logging), connectivity. - ☐ **Common transducers:** Thermocouple (mV/°C), RTD ($\Omega$ vs T), LVDT (V vs displacement), Strain gauge (resistance change), Piezoelectric (charge/force). - ☐ **Always state UNITS** in numerical answers — marks are lost for "LSB = 4.88" without specifying mV or $\mu$V. - ☐ **Bipolar ADC formula:** $\text{Code} = (V_{in}/\Delta) + 2^{n-1}$ — do not forget the offset $2^{n-1}$. - ☐ **LabVIEW VIs** have two windows: **Front Panel** (UI) and **Block Diagram** (graphical code) — both must appear in exam answers when asked.

topics.title: Image acquisition techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL607 - MECHATRONICS SYSTEMS & PLC LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Image acquisition techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mechatronics Systems & Machine Vision**, **Image Acquisition** is defined as the systematic process of capturing, converting, and digitally encoding a real-world 2D or 3D visual scene into a discrete numerical representation (pixel matrix) that can be subsequently processed, analyzed, and interpreted by an embedded computing unit (PC, microcontroller, DSP, or FPGA).

Mathematically, an acquired grayscale image is represented as a discrete function:

$$I(x, y): \mathbb{Z}^2 \rightarrow \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$$

where $(x, y)$ denotes the integer spatial coordinates of a **pixel** (picture element), and the output value $I(x,y)$ represents the quantized **intensity** (brightness) at that location. For color images, a third channel dimension (RGB) is added, yielding a tensor:

$$I(x, y, c) \quad \text{where} \quad c \in \{R, G, B\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Image acquisition is the *first and most critical stage* in any machine vision pipeline. KTU examiners frequently state that "garbage in = garbage out" — if the acquired image is poor, no downstream algorithm (edge detection, segmentation, CNN classification) can recover meaningful information.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of image acquisition like **filling a bucket with raindrops to measure rainfall intensity**.

- The **sky (scene)** produces continuous "rain" (light rays reflected from objects).
- The **bucket opening (camera sensor)** has a fixed finite area — only drops falling into it can be measured.
- The **bucket wall (lens + aperture)** controls *how many* drops (amount of light) and *which directions* are admitted.
- Finally, the **measuring tube (ADC – Analog-to-Digital Converter)** counts and discretizes the collected water into measurable cup levels (pixel intensity values 0–255).

Just as a *narrower bucket captures fewer drops but yields sharper local measurements*, a **smaller aperture** in a camera allows less light but produces a **deeper depth of field** and sharper focus.

## 1.3 Core Components of an Image Acquisition System

An image acquisition chain in mechatronics consists of five essential building blocks:

1. **Illumination Source** (LED ring, backlight, laser, halogen)
2. **Optical Lens System** (focal length, aperture, FOV)
3. **Image Sensor** (CCD or CMOS array)
4. **Frame Grabber / Interface** (USB3, GigE, Camera Link, MIPI CSI)
5. **Host Processing Unit** (PC / PLC / embedded controller)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics** (commonly tested in KTU):
> - **Resolution:** measured in **megapixels (MP)** or **pixels per mm (px/mm)**
> - **Frame Rate:** measured in **frames per second (fps)**
> - **Dynamic Range:** measured in **decibels (dB)** or **bits** (typical: 8-bit, 12-bit, 14-bit)
> - **Sensor Format:** measured in **inches** (e.g., 1/3", 1/2", 2/3")
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Image Acquisition Pipeline

### Step 1: Illumination Engineering
Light is projected onto the workpiece. The choice of illumination determines **contrast**, **shadow formation**, and **specular reflection suppression**.

| Lighting Type | Application | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Front / Ring Light** | Diffuse illumination | Surface inspection, PCB checking |
| **Backlight** | Silhouette formation | Edge profiling, hole/gap measurement |
| **Structured Light** | Projected pattern | 3D depth reconstruction |
| **Coaxial Light** | Eliminates shadows | Reflective surface (mirror, glass) |
| **Dome Light** | Uniform scattered light | Curved / shiny metallic parts |

### Step 2: Optical Imaging via Lens
The lens bends incoming light rays according to the **thin lens equation**:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$$

where:
- $f$ = focal length (mm)
- $u$ = object distance from lens (mm)
- $v$ = image distance from lens (mm)

### Step 3: Sensor Conversion (Photon → Electron → Voltage)
- **Photons** strike the silicon photodiodes in the sensor.
- The **photoelectric effect** liberates electrons proportional to light intensity.
- Each pixel's charge is read out, **amplified**, and **digitized** by an on-chip ADC.

### Step 4: Signal Transmission
Digital pixel data is serialized and transmitted over the interface (USB3 Vision, GigE Vision, MIPI CSI-2, etc.) to the host.

### Step 5: Pre-processing (Optional Onboard)
Bayer demosaicing, white balance, gamma correction, and noise filtering occur before storage in RAM.

## 2.2 Camera Sensor Technologies: CCD vs CMOS

| Parameter | **CCD (Charge Coupled Device)** | **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide Semiconductor)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Readout Mechanism** | Sequential bucket-brigade charge transfer | Per-pixel independent transistor readout |
| **Noise** | Very low (high SNR) | Higher (active transistors per pixel) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (clock signals across array) | Low (10–100× lower) |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Very high (global + rolling shutter options) |
| **Cost** | Expensive | Cheap (fabricated in standard semiconductor fabs) |
| **Application** | Scientific, astronomy, microscopy | Industrial robotics, automotive, IoT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Modern industrial mechatronics systems **overwhelmingly prefer CMOS** sensors (e.g., Sony IMX series, ON Semiconductor AR) due to their lower power, faster frame rates, and integrated on-chip processing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$ | Thin lens equation | mm |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{v}{u}$ | Linear magnification (image size / object size) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{FOV}_w = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_h}{2}\right)$ | Field of view (width) | mm |
| 4 | $\text{FOV}_h = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_v}{2}\right)$ | Field of view (height) | mm |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{f}{D}$ | F-number (aperture) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{N_{\text{pixels}}}$ | Spatial resolution (mm/pixel) | mm/pixel |
| 7 | $\text{DOF} \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot N \cdot c \cdot (1 + M)}{M^2}$ | Depth of field (approximation) | mm |
| 8 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 9 | $I_t = I_0 \cdot 10^{-OD}$ | Beer-Lambert transmittance | – |

> [!WARNING]
> Always avoid using the vertical bar `\|` symbol in markdown tables (it breaks the column parser). Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Image acquisition is the enabling technology for:

- **Automated Optical Inspection (AOI)** in SMT electronics assembly
- **Pick-and-place robotics** with visual servoing
- **Quality control** in food and pharmaceutical industries
- **Autonomous vehicles** (lane detection, obstacle recognition)
- **Medical imaging** (endoscopy, X-ray digitization)
- **Agricultural drones** (crop health monitoring via NDVI imaging)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Field of View (FOV) from Sensor Geometry and Working Distance

**Given:**
- Sensor width: $W_s$ (mm)
- Sensor height: $H_s$ (mm)
- Focal length: $f$ (mm)
- Working distance (object-to-lens distance): $d$ (mm)

**Find:** Horizontal field of view $\text{FOV}_w$ and vertical field of view $\text{FOV}_h$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** From the thin lens geometry, the half-angle of view on the sensor side is:

$$\tan(\theta_h / 2) = \frac{W_s / 2}{f}$$

The reasoning: the sensor width spans from $-W_s/2$ to $+W_s/2$ relative to the optical axis, and the focal length $f$ is the perpendicular distance to the principal plane. Hence the tangent relation follows directly from similar triangles.

**Step 2:** Apply the principle of similar triangles on the object side to obtain the linear extent visible at distance $d$:

$$\frac{\text{FOV}_w / 2}{d} = \frac{W_s / 2}{f}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\frac{\text{FOV}_w}{d} = \frac{W_s}{f}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\text{FOV}_w$:

$$\boxed{\text{FOV}_w = \frac{W_s \cdot d}{f}}$$

**Step 4:** By identical logic for the vertical axis:

$$\boxed{\text{FOV}_h = \frac{H_s \cdot d}{f}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example:

Suppose a **2/3-inch sensor** (approximately $W_s = 8.8$ mm, $H_s = 6.6$ mm) with a **$f = 8$ mm lens** is placed at working distance **$d = 300$ mm** from a workpiece.

**Step A — Horizontal FOV:**

$$\text{FOV}_w = \frac{8.8 \times 300}{8} = 330 \text{ mm}$$

**Step B — Vertical FOV:**

$$\text{FOV}_h = \frac{6.6 \times 300}{8} = 247.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step C — Spatial Resolution (pitch):**
If the camera outputs **$1920 \times 1080$ pixels**:

$$\text{Pitch}_w = \frac{330}{1920} \approx 0.172 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

$$\text{Pitch}_h = \frac{247.5}{1080} \approx 0.229 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: F-Number (N) and Its Effect on Depth of Field

The **F-number** $N$ is defined as:

$$N = \frac{f}{D}$$

where $D$ is the effective aperture diameter.

The reasoning: a longer focal length $f$ requires a proportionally larger aperture to admit the same cone angle of light, and the F-number normalizes this ratio for ease of comparison.

The **depth of field (DOF)** is the range of object distances that remain in acceptable focus. The standard approximate formula is:

$$\text{DOF} \approx \frac{2 \cdot N \cdot c \cdot (1 + M)}{M^2}$$

where:
- $N$ = F-number
- $c$ = circle of confusion (typically **0.03 mm** for a 1/2" sensor)
- $M$ = magnification

### Numerical Validation:

Take $N = 8$, $c = 0.03$ mm, $M = 0.5$.

$$\text{DOF} = \frac{2 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (1 + 0.5)}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{2 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 1.5}{0.25}$$

$$\text{DOF} = \frac{0.72}{0.25} = 2.88 \text{ mm}$$

This means objects from approximately **2.88 mm** of axial distance will appear sharp — useful for small-part inspection.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Image Sampling and Nyquist Criterion

To faithfully capture a feature, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest spatial frequency present (Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem):

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

In practical machine vision terms, this translates to: **at least 2 pixels must cover the smallest resolvable feature**.

$$\text{Required pitch} \leq \frac{\text{Smallest feature size}}{2}$$

If the smallest defect is **0.1 mm**, the required pixel pitch is $\leq 0.05$ mm/pixel.

For the earlier example with pitch $\approx 0.172$ mm/pixel, we can resolve features down to $\approx 0.344$ mm — features smaller than that will be aliased or missed.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Lens Selection Decision Flow

| Application Need | Recommended Focal Length | Lighting Type |
|---|---|---|
| Large area, distant object ($d > 1$ m) | $f = 8$–$25$ mm | Front diffuse |
| Small part inspection ($d < 200$ mm) | $f = 25$–$75$ mm macro | Backlight |
| 3D profiling | $f = 8$–$16$ mm | Structured light / laser line |
| High-speed conveyor | $f = 12$–$35$ mm | Strobe / pulsed LED |

## 3.5 Calibration Equation: Pixel-to-Millimeter Mapping

For a calibrated camera system, the conversion factor (mm/pixel) is determined experimentally:

$$k = \frac{L_{real}}{L_{pixels}}$$

The reasoning: place a calibration target of known real-world length $L_{real}$ (e.g., a checkerboard square of 10 mm) in the field of view. Count the number of pixels $L_{pixels}$ it occupies. The ratio $k$ is the calibration constant, used thereafter to convert any measured pixel distance back to real-world units.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    C --> D[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]

    F --> G[Frame Buffer RAM]
    G --> H[Host PC or PLC]
    H --> I[Image Processing Algorithm]
    I --> J[Decision Actuator Output]

```

```mermaid
    Q2{Object Moving?} -->|Yes| P4[Use Global Shutter CMOS]

    Q3{Surface Reflective?} -->|Yes| P6[Coaxial or Polarized Light]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define image acquisition in the context of machine vision. List any four key parameters that characterize an image acquisition system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Image acquisition is the process of capturing a real-world visual scene using an optical sensor and converting it into a digital pixel matrix that can be processed by a computer.

Four key parameters:

1. **Resolution** — number of pixels (e.g., 1920 × 1080); determines smallest detectable feature.
2. **Frame rate** — frames per second (fps); determines ability to capture motion.
3. **Sensor type** — CCD or CMOS; affects noise, speed, and cost.
4. **Dynamic range** — ratio of maximum to minimum detectable intensity (typically 8–14 bits).

*(Award 0.5 marks for definition + 0.5 marks × 4 for parameters = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between front lighting and backlighting techniques used in machine vision.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Front Lighting | Back Lighting |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Position** | Same side as camera | Opposite side of object |
| **Result** | Highlights surface texture | Forms a silhouette |
| **Best For** | Defect, color, text inspection | Edge measurement, hole/gap detection |
| **Contrast Type** | Reflective (surface) | Transmissive (geometry) |
| **Shadow Formation** | Yes | None |

*(Award 1.5 marks for tabular comparison + 1.5 marks for explanation = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (Option 1): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**A mechatronics inspection station uses a 1/2-inch CMOS camera (sensor size 6.4 mm × 4.8 mm) equipped with a 12 mm focal length lens. The object is placed 250 mm from the lens. The camera outputs images of 1600 × 1200 pixels.**

#### (a) Compute the field of view (horizontal and vertical) in mm. (7 marks)

#### (b) Determine the spatial resolution (mm/pixel) in both directions. If the smallest defect to be detected is 0.2 mm, comment on whether this setup satisfies the Nyquist criterion. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Field of View Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify known quantities:**

- $W_s = 6.4$ mm (sensor width)
- $H_s = 4.8$ mm (sensor height)
- $f = 12$ mm (focal length)
- $d = 250$ mm (working distance)

**Step 2 — Apply FOV formula:**

$$\text{FOV}_w = \frac{W_s \cdot d}{f} = \frac{6.4 \times 250}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\boxed{\text{FOV}_w = 133.33 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Vertical FOV:**

$$\text{FOV}_h = \frac{H_s \cdot d}{f} = \frac{4.8 \times 250}{12}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FOV}_h = 100.00 \text{ mm}}$$

*(Award 2 marks formula + 2 marks horizontal + 1 mark vertical + 2 marks units = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Spatial Resolution and Nyquist Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute pixel pitch (horizontal):**

$$\text{Pitch}_w = \frac{\text{FOV}_w}{N_{w}} = \frac{133.33}{1600} = 0.0833 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pixel pitch (vertical):**

$$\text{Pitch}_h = \frac{\text{FOV}_h}{N_{h}} = \frac{100.00}{1200} = 0.0833 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective feature size on sensor (worst case = larger pitch):**

$$\text{Effective pitch} = 0.0833 \text{ mm/pixel}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Nyquist criterion:**

The smallest resolvable feature size must be **at least 2 × pixel pitch**:

$$\text{Min resolvable feature} = 2 \times 0.0833 = 0.1667 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with required defect size:**

$$\text{Required} = 0.2 \text{ mm} \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{Achieved} = 0.1667 \text{ mm}$$

Since $0.1667 \text{ mm} < 0.2 \text{ mm}$, **the setup DOES satisfy the Nyquist criterion**, and the 0.2 mm defect can be reliably detected.

*(Award 2 marks pitch calculation + 2 marks Nyquist comparison + 2 marks conclusion + 1 mark for unit consistency = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (Option 2): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Retest]`

**With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the functional stages of a CMOS image acquisition system. Compare the performance of CCD and CMOS sensors in industrial vision applications.**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part 1 — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

A CMOS image acquisition system consists of the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1: Optical Stage**
- Light from the illuminated object passes through the **lens assembly**.
- The lens focuses the light onto the **photodiode array** of the sensor.

**Stage 2: Photon-to-Electron Conversion**
- Each pixel contains a **photodiode** that converts incident photons into electrons via the **photoelectric effect**.
- The accumulated charge is proportional to light intensity.

**Stage 3: Charge-to-Voltage Conversion**
- Each pixel has its own **amplifier (source follower)** that converts the stored charge into a voltage signal.

**Stage 4: Readout and Multiplexing**
- A **row select transistor** activates a row of pixels at a time.
- The **column multiplexer** routes selected pixel voltages to a common output bus.

**Stage 5: Analog-to-Digital Conversion**
- An on-chip **ADC** (typically 10–14 bit) digitizes the analog voltage into a binary code.

**Stage 6: Image Signal Processing (ISP)**
- Performs **Bayer demosaicing**, **white balance**, **noise reduction**, **gamma correction**.

**Stage 7: Output Interface**
- Digital frames are transmitted via **MIPI CSI-2, USB3 Vision, or GigE** to the host processor.

*(Award 1 mark per stage × 7 stages = 7 marks)*

---

**Part 2 — CCD vs CMOS Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | CCD | CMOS |
|---|---|---|
| **Fill Factor** | High (>90%) | Lower (30–60%) due to per-pixel transistors |
| **Noise Performance** | Excellent (low read noise) | Moderate (active transistors inject noise) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (clock signals for charge transfer) | Low (10–100× less) |
| **Frame Rate** | Moderate | Very high (parallel readout) |
| **Shutter Type** | Global shutter (typical) | Rolling or global (sensor-dependent) |
| **Cost** | High (specialized fab) | Low (standard CMOS process) |
| **On-chip Integration** | Limited | High (ADC, ISP, timing all integrated) |
| **Industrial Preference** | Scientific / low-light applications | Robotics, AOI, automotive, IoT |

**Conclusion for industrial use:**
**CMOS is preferred** in modern mechatronics due to its lower cost, lower power, higher speed, and integration of on-chip processing. CCD is reserved for niche low-light or scientific imaging.

*(Award 1 mark per row × 6 rows + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit** consistently. Always write `mm`, `fps`, `mm/pixel`.
> 2. **Wrong formula application** — Students often confuse the **FOV** formula $\text{FOV} = W_s \cdot d / f$ with the **magnification** formula $M = v / u$. These are NOT interchangeable.
> 3. **Ignoring the Nyquist criterion** — When asked "can a defect of X mm be detected?", you MUST apply the $2 \times$ pixel pitch rule, not just compare directly.
> 4. **Mixing up CCD/CMOS** — A common error is stating CMOS has lower noise than CCD; the reverse is true.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram in 14-mark questions** — Always include a labelled diagram; it carries **2–3 marks** even if text is brief.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Image acquisition** = capturing a scene as a 2D digital pixel matrix $I(x, y)$.
- [x] Five-stage pipeline: **Illumination → Lens → Sensor → Digitization → Processing**.
- [x] **Thin lens equation**: $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$.
- [x] **FOV formula** (most tested): $\text{FOV} = \dfrac{W_s \cdot d}{f}$.
- [x] **Magnification** $M = v / u$.
- [x] **F-number**: $N = f / D$.
- [x] **Pixel pitch** (spatial resolution): $\text{Pitch} = \text{FOV} / N_{\text{pixels}}$.
- [x] **Nyquist rule**: smallest feature $\geq 2 \times$ pixel pitch.
- [x] **Depth of field** depends on $N$, $c$, and $M$.
- [x] **CCD** = sequential readout, low noise, high power.
- [x] **CMOS** = per-pixel readout, higher noise, low power, **preferred in industry**.
- [x] **Front light** → surface defects; **Back light** → geometric edges.
- [x] **Global shutter** required for moving objects; rolling shutter OK for static scenes.
- [x] Standard interfaces: **USB3 Vision, GigE Vision, MIPI CSI-2, Camera Link**.
- [x] Always quote **units** (mm, fps, dB, bits) — KTU deducts marks otherwise.
- [x] Calibration constant $k = L_{real} / L_{pixels}$ converts pixels to mm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of stepper motor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL607 - MECHATRONICS SYSTEMS & PLC LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control of stepper motor

---

# ⚙️ Control of Stepper Motor — KTU Lab Master Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **stepper motor** is a *brushless, synchronous electric motor* that converts a series of electrical pulses applied to its stator windings into discrete angular increments called *steps*. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular displacement known as the **step angle (θs)**, making it an *open-loop* positioning actuator — the most common precision motion element in Mechatronics laboratories.

### 🔑 Key Operating Principle

The rotor aligns itself to the magnetic axis of the *energized* stator phase. By sequentially energizing the stator coils in a specific order, the rotor "steps" from one stable position to the next. Unlike a DC motor (which spins freely), the stepper motor holds its position firmly when the last pulse ends — this is its signature trait.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 lab manual, *Module Experiment 2* focuses on interfacing a stepper motor to a microcontroller/PLC trainer kit, generating the **phase excitation sequence**, and verifying **direction reversal**, **speed variation** (via pulse frequency), and **angle control** (via pulse count).

### 🍕 Real-World Analogy — "The Pizza Cutter"

Imagine a pizza shop wheel running on a **notched track**:
- Each notch on the track = one **step angle**.
- A worker pushing the wheel one notch at a time = one **pulse**.
- Push left-to-right → motor rotates *clockwise*. Push right-to-left → *anticlockwise*.
- Push faster → motor **speeds up**.
- Stop pushing → wheel **locks in place** (holding torque).

That's exactly how a stepper motor behaves — it moves in **discrete steps**, holds its position when stopped, and reverses by flipping the excitation sequence.

### 📐 Fundamental Physical Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Step Angle | $\theta_s$ | **1.8°** (most common), 0.72°, 7.5°, 15° |
| Steps per Revolution | $N$ | **200** (for 1.8°), 48, 500 |
| Holding Torque | $T_h$ | **0.1 – 10 Nm** (lab scale) |
| Operating Voltage | $V$ | **5 V DC**, 12 V DC |
| Phase Current | $I$ | **0.5 – 2 A** per phase |

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between step angle and steps per revolution is given by:
>
> $$N = \frac{360°}{\theta_s}$$
>
> For $\theta_s = 1.8°$ → $N = 200$ steps/rev. This number is the **foundation of every KTU numerical** in this experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Stepper Motor

A stepper motor consists of two main parts:

- **Stator (Stationary part):** Comprises multiple electromagnets (phases). Common configurations are **2-phase**, **4-phase**, or **5-phase**. Lab motors are typically **4-phase unipolar** (5 or 6 wires) or **2-phase bipolar** (4 wires).
- **Rotor (Rotating part):** Either a **Permanent Magnet (PM)** rotor (low cost, used in lab kits) or a **Variable Reluctance (VR)** rotor (no magnet, uses toothed iron core).

### 2.2 The Two Dominant Construction Types

| Type | Construction | Lab Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Toothed soft-iron rotor; rotates to minimize magnetic reluctance | Rare in labs |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized ferrite rotor, multi-toothed | Most common (e.g., 1.8° motors) |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | Combines PM + toothed structure; smallest step angles | High-precision CNC |

### 2.3 Excitation Sequences — The Heart of the Experiment

The KTU lab focuses on three driving modes:

#### (a) Wave Drive (One-Phase-On) — Full Step
Only **one phase is energized at a time**. Gives minimum torque but lowest power consumption.

$$T_{wave} \approx 0.707 \cdot T_{hold}$$

#### (b) Full-Step Drive (Two-Phase-On)
**Two adjacent phases are energized simultaneously**. This is the **default robust mode** used in lab trainers because it produces the maximum holding torque.

$$T_{full} \approx 1.0 \cdot T_{hold}$$

> [!TIP]
> In full-step two-phase-on mode, the rotor sits at the **midpoint between two teeth** of adjacent energized phases — producing roughly **40% more torque** than wave drive.

#### (c) Half-Step Drive (Alternate)
Alternates between one-phase-on and two-phase-on, **doubling the resolution**.

$$\theta_{s,\,half} = \frac{\theta_s}{2} = 0.9° \text{ (for a 1.8° motor)}$$

Steps per revolution double: $N_{half} = 400$ steps/rev.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers **every numerical** the examiner can ask.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steps per revolution | $N = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | dimensionless | Foundation formula |
| 2 | Step angle (half-step) | $\theta_{s/2} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{2}$ | degrees | Half-step mode |
| 3 | Shaft rotation angle | $\theta = N_{pulses} \cdot \theta_s$ | degrees | $N_{pulses}$ = pulses sent |
| 4 | Pulses required | $N_{pulses} = \dfrac{\theta_{desired}}{\theta_s}$ | pulses | Inverse of (3) |
| 5 | Shaft speed (RPM) | $n = \dfrac{f}{N}$ | rev/min | $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz) |
| 6 | Shaft speed (rad/s) | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{N}$ | rad/s | Converted form |
| 7 | Step frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz | $T$ = time between pulses |
| 8 | Resolution (half-step) | $N_{half} = 2N$ | steps/rev | Halve step angle |
| 9 | Gear-down output angle | $\theta_{out} = \dfrac{\theta_{in}}{G}$ | degrees | $G$ = gear ratio |
| 10 | Holding torque relation | $T \propto I \cdot N_{turns}$ | N·m | For coil design |

### 2.5 Driving Electronics — The Driver IC

A microcontroller (or PLC) cannot source enough current to drive the motor coils directly. Hence a **driver stage** is mandatory. The lab typically uses:

- **ULN2003** — Darlington array (7 channels) for **unipolar** 5-wire/6-wire motors.
- **L293D / L298N** — H-Bridge IC for **bipolar** 4-wire motors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a driver?** The I/O pin of an 8051/Arduino/PLC outputs only ~20 mA, but a stepper coil demands **300 mA – 2 A**. The driver acts as a *current amplifier* and *protection barrier*, isolating the logic side from the inductive kickback of the coils (freewheeling diodes inside ULN2003 handle this).

### 2.6 Why Direction Reversal Works

The rotor follows the **rotating magnetic field** created by the stator. Reversing the excitation sequence (e.g., A → B → C → D becomes A → D → C → B) reverses the field rotation, and hence the rotor direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's golden line:** *"Direction reversal in a stepper motor is achieved by reversing the order of phase excitation — no polarity change of supply is needed."*

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **3D Printers** — X/Y/Z axis positioning using NEMA 17 steppers.
- **CNC Machines** — Tool head and bed positioning.
- **Robotic Joints** — Open-loop angular control.
- **Hard Disk Drives** — Head positioning actuator.
- **Medical Syringe Pumps** — Precise fluid delivery.
- **Lab Trainers (KTU)** — Where you stand today.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Step Angle and Steps per Revolution

A stepper motor advances by a fixed angle $\theta_s$ for each pulse. After $N$ pulses, it completes one full mechanical revolution of $360°$.

By definition of uniform stepping:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N \cdot \theta_s &= 360° \\[4pt]
\theta_s &= \frac{360°}{N} \\[4pt]
N &= \frac{360°}{\theta_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Total angular displacement = (number of steps) × (angle per step). Setting this equal to one revolution gives the closed-form identity. For a 1.8° motor, $N = 360/1.8 = 200$ steps.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Pulses Required to Rotate Shaft by Angle $\theta_{desired}$

Given a desired rotation $\theta_{desired}$ (in degrees), and a known step angle $\theta_s$, the number of electrical pulses to send is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Angle per pulse} &= \theta_s \\
\text{Total pulses} &= \frac{\text{Total desired angle}}{\text{Angle per pulse}} \\[4pt]
\therefore N_{pulses} &= \frac{\theta_{desired}}{\theta_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 1.8° stepper must rotate by **90°**. How many pulses?

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{pulses} &= \frac{90°}{1.8°} \\[4pt]
&= 50 \text{ pulses}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shaft Speed vs Pulse Frequency

If pulses are delivered at frequency $f$ (pulses per second = Hz), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time per step} &= T = \frac{1}{f} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
\text{Steps per second} &= \frac{N_{pulses}}{T} = f \\[4pt]
\text{Steps per minute} &= 60 f \\[4pt]
\text{Revolutions per minute (RPM)} &= \frac{60 f}{N} \\[4pt]
\therefore n &= \frac{60 f}{N} = \frac{f}{N} \text{ (in rev/sec, multiply by 60 for RPM)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compact form (two equivalent versions):**

$$
n = \frac{60 \, f}{N} \quad \text{RPM} \qquad \Longleftrightarrow \qquad n = \frac{f}{N} \text{ rev/sec}
$$

**Numerical Example:** $f = 500$ Hz, $N = 200$ steps/rev.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{60 \times 500}{200} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{30000}{200} \\[4pt]
&= 150 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Angular Velocity in rad/s

Converting RPM to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi n}{60} \\[4pt]
\omega &= \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{60 f}{N} \\[4pt]
\therefore \omega &= \frac{2\pi f}{N} \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical form** — directly ties electrical pulse frequency to mechanical angular velocity.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Half-Step Resolution

In half-step mode, the excitation alternates between one-phase-on and two-phase-on, halving the effective step angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{s,\,half} &= \frac{\theta_s}{2} \\[4pt]
N_{half} &= \frac{360°}{\theta_{s,\,half}} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s / 2} = \frac{2 \times 360°}{\theta_s} = 2N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $\theta_s = 1.8°$ → $\theta_{s,half} = 0.9°$ → $N_{half} = 400$ steps/rev.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Total Time to Rotate by Angle $\theta_{desired}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of pulses} &= N_{pulses} = \frac{\theta_{desired}}{\theta_s} \\[4pt]
\text{Time per pulse} &= T = \frac{1}{f} \\[4pt]
\therefore t_{total} &= N_{pulses} \cdot T = \frac{\theta_{desired}}{\theta_s \cdot f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Rotate 90° at 500 Hz with 1.8° stepper:

$$
t_{total} = \frac{90°}{1.8° \times 500} = \frac{90}{900} = 0.1 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Excitation Mode

| Criterion | Wave Drive | Full-Step (2-phase) | Half-Step |
|---|---|---|---|
| Torque output | Low (~70%) | **High (100%)** | Medium (~85%) |
| Power consumption | **Lowest** | High | Medium |
| Resolution | $N$ steps/rev | $N$ steps/rev | **$2N$ steps/rev** |
| Smoothness | Jerky | Jerky | **Smoothest** |
| Heat in coils | Least | Most | Medium |
| **KTU lab default** | Sometimes | **Yes — most common** | Optional advanced |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU lab record, always **default to Full-Step Two-Phase-On** unless the question explicitly asks for half-step. The driver IC ULN2003 is wired for this mode by default.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL UNIT"]
        MCU["Microcontroller or PLC Trainer"]
        PGEN["Pulse Generator Logic"]
        SEQ["Phase Sequence Lookup Table"]
    end

    subgraph DRV["DRIVER STAGE"]
        DRVIC["Driver IC ULN2003 or L293D"]
        PROT["Freewheeling Diodes and Snubber"]
    end

    subgraph MOT["ACTUATOR"]
        COILA["Phase A Coil"]
        COILB["Phase B Coil"]
        COILC["Phase C Coil"]
        COILD["Phase D Coil"]
        ROT["Permanent Magnet Rotor"]
    end

    subgraph FB["FEEDBACK"]
        OBS["Shaft Position Observer"]
        IND["LED Indicator Ring"]
    end

    MCU --> PGEN
    PGEN --> SEQ
    SEQ --> DRVIC
    DRVIC --> PROT
    PROT --> COILA
    PROT --> COILB
    PROT --> COILC
    PROT --> COILD
    COILA --> ROT
    COILB --> ROT
    COILC --> ROT
    COILD --> ROT
    ROT --> OBS
    OBS --> IND
```

### 4.2 Phase Excitation Sequence — Wave Drive (4-Phase Motor)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as Clock Pulse
    participant SEQ as Sequencer
    participant A as Phase A
    participant B as Phase B
    participant C as Phase C
    participant D as Phase D

    CLK->>SEQ: Pulse 1
    SEQ->>A: ON
    Note over B,C,D: OFF

    CLK->>SEQ: Pulse 2
    SEQ->>B: ON
    Note over A,C,D: OFF

    CLK->>SEQ: Pulse 3
    SEQ->>C: ON
    Note over A,B,D: OFF

    CLK->>SEQ: Pulse 4
    SEQ->>D: ON
    Note over A,B,C: OFF

    CLK->>SEQ: Pulse 5
    SEQ->>A: ON
    Note right of SEQ: Cycle Repeats CW
```

### 4.3 Half-Step Drive — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start]) --> STEP1["Step 1: A ON"]
    STEP1 --> STEP2["Step 2: A + B ON"]
    STEP2 --> STEP3["Step 3: B ON"]

    STEP4 --> STEP5["Step 5: C ON"]
    STEP5 --> STEP6["Step 6: C + D ON"]
    STEP6 --> STEP7["Step 7: D ON"]
    STEP7 --> STEP8["Step 8: D + A ON"]
    STEP8 --> DECIDE{Direction Flag}
    DECIDE -- CW --> STEP1
    DECIDE -- CCW --> STEP8
```

### 4.4 Hardware Interfacing Schematic (Block View)

```mermaid

    end

    subgraph P2["ULN2003 Driver IC"]

    end

    subgraph P3["Stepper Motor"]
        P3A["Coil A Terminal"]
```
