> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment maps to **CO2 — Design and implement PLC-based control logic for sequential and continuous industrial processes** and **CO3 — Apply sensor–actuator interfacing techniques in mechatronic systems** of PCMRL607.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Think of the system as a **modern automatic flush toilet tank**:

- The **float ball** in a toilet is analogous to a **level sensor** in our experiment.
- The **fill valve** that opens/closes is analogous to a **solenoid valve / pump** controlled by the PLC.
- The **brain of the operation** (your hand pulling the flush lever) is replaced by the **PLC** executing ladder-logic rungs every few milliseconds.

Whenever the water falls below a setpoint, the tank fills; once the upper limit is reached, filling stops automatically — no human intervention required. The PLC performs this same decision-making thousands of times faster and with industrial-grade reliability.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **g = 9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration, used in hydrostatic head calculations).
- **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³** (standard reference density for water-based experiments).
- **Standard Industrial Levels:** LOW (L), MID (M), HIGH (H) — typically three discrete sensor thresholds.
- **Scan Cycle Time:** **10–20 ms** for compact PLCs (Siemens S7-1200 / Allen-Bradley MicroLogix).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab evaluations, students are expected to **document the I/O list**, the **ladder program**, the **timing diagram**, and a **working demonstration** of level maintenance.

### 1.4 Components of the Experimental Setup

| Sl. No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Tank | Stores the liquid (water) |
| 2 | Level Sensors (Float / Conductive / Capacitive) | Detect LOW, MID, HIGH states |
| 3 | Inlet Pump (or Solenoid Valve) | Fills the tank |
| 4 | Outlet Pump (or Drain Valve) | Drains the tank |
| 5 | PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-1200 / Allen-Bradley) | Executes control logic |
| 6 | Power Supply (24 V DC) | Powers sensors & relay coils |
| 7 | HMI / Indicator Lamps | Displays system status |
| 8 | Push Buttons (START / STOP) | Manual override |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The control strategy can be decomposed into **four discrete logic blocks**:

1. **Sensing Block:** Level sensors convert the physical liquid height into discrete electrical signals (ON/OFF for digital sensors, or 4–20 mA / 0–10 V for analog sensors).
2. **Decision Block:** The PLC scans its inputs every scan cycle (typically **≤ 10 ms**) and evaluates the ladder program rung-by-rung. Based on input combinations, it decides which outputs to energize.
3. **Actuation Block:** Energized output contacts drive relay coils, which in turn switch ON/OFF pumps or solenoid valves.
4. **Feedback Block:** The new liquid level is sensed again, closing the loop.

### 2.2 Control Strategies (Most KTU questions are based on these)

**A. Two-Point (ON/OFF) Control — Most Common in KTU Labs**
- Uses **two threshold sensors**: LOW sensor (LSL) and HIGH sensor (LSH).
- Inlet opens when level ≤ LOW, closes when level ≥ HIGH.
- **Hysteresis band** = (HIGH setpoint − LOW setpoint) prevents relay chattering.

**B. Three-Point (Sequential) Control**
- Uses LOW, MID, HIGH sensors.
- Differential control with multiple operating modes (Fill / Hold / Drain).

**C. Analog (PID) Control**
- Uses continuous level transmitter (e.g., 4–20 mA proportional to 0–100% level).
- PLC computes error *e(t)* and applies **PID algorithm** to drive a variable-speed pump (VFD) or proportional valve.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa (N/m²) | Sensor calibration |
| Tank Volume | $V = A \cdot h = \pi \cdot r^{2} \cdot h$ | m³ | Capacity calculation |
| Level Error (Analog) | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | % or m | PID input |
| PID Output | $u(t) = K_{p}\!\left(e + \frac{1}{T_{i}}\!\int e\,dt + T_{d}\frac{de}{dt}\right)$ | % | Continuous control |
| Hysteresis Band | $\Delta h = h_{HIGH} - h_{LOW}$ | m | Anti-chatter design |
| Flow Rate (Continuity) | $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}$ | m³/s | Dynamic modeling |
| Scan Time Constraint | $t_{scan} \le 0.1 \cdot T_{process}$ | s | PLC selection rule |

> [!NOTE]
> The **continuity equation** $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt}$ is the **dynamic model** of the tank. KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive this in viva voce.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water treatment plants** — chemical dosing tanks.
- **Oil refineries** — separator and surge tanks.
- **Dairy & beverage industry** — CIP (Clean-In-Place) tanks.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — mixing vessels with strict level tolerances.
- **Power plants** — demineralized water storage, boiler feed tanks.

The same ladder logic built for the lab experiment scales directly to **SCADA-supervised DCS** (Distributed Control Systems) used in refineries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tank Dynamic Model (Continuity Equation)

The continuity equation is the **mathematical heart** of any level-control problem. KTU examiners expect students to derive it in the lab record.

**Step 1 — State the principle of conservation of mass.**

The net mass flowing into the tank per unit time equals the rate of change of mass stored inside.

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass flow to volumetric flow using density.**

Assuming the liquid is **incompressible** (water, ρ = constant):

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = \rho \cdot Q_{in} - \rho \cdot Q_{out}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the tank volume $V = A \cdot h$ (assuming constant cross-section).**

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine all terms and cancel the constant density.**

$$
\rho \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} = \rho \cdot Q_{in} - \rho \cdot Q_{out}
$$

$$
A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}
$$

**Final Governing Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{in} - Q_{out} = A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation for KTU viva:**

- If $Q_{in} > Q_{out}$ → $\dfrac{dh}{dt} > 0$ → Level rises.
- If $Q_{in} < Q_{out}$ → $\dfrac{dh}{dt} < 0$ → Level falls.
- If $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$ → $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = 0$ → Level is steady (equilibrium).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Ladder Logic for Two-Point Control

**I/O Assignment (Assumed for a typical KTU lab rig):**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START Push Button (NO) | Input |
| I0.1 | STOP Push Button (NC) | Input |
| I0.2 | LOW Level Sensor (LSL) | Input |
| I0.3 | HIGH Level Sensor (LSH) | Input |
| Q0.0 | Inlet Pump / Solenoid | Output |
| Q0.1 | Outlet Pump | Output |
| M0.0 | RUN Latching Coil | Internal Memory |

**Ladder Rungs (Logical Form):**

**Rung 1 — Master Latch (RUN condition):**

$$
\text{RUN} = (\text{START} + \text{RUN}) \cdot \overline{\text{STOP}} \cdot \overline{\text{LSH\_Fault}}
$$

**Rung 2 — Inlet Pump Control:**

$$
Q_{0.0} = \text{RUN} \cdot \overline{I_{0.3}} \cdot \left( \overline{I_{0.2}} + Q_{0.0} \right)
$$

Interpretation: *Inlet pump runs when system is RUN, level is below HIGH sensor, and either LOW sensor is active OR inlet was already running.*

**Rung 3 — Outlet Pump Control (Drain on demand):**

$$
Q_{0.1} = \text{RUN} \cdot I_{0.3} \cdot \overline{I_{0.2}}
$$

Interpretation: *Drain only when level is above HIGH and LOW sensor is NOT active (safety — prevents dry-run).*

### 3.3 Truth Table Analysis (Two-Point ON/OFF Control)

| I0.2 (LOW) | I0.3 (HIGH) | RUN (M0.0) | Inlet (Q0.0) | Outlet (Q0.1) | Tank State |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **ON** | OFF | Empty — Filling |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **ON** | OFF | Mid — Still Filling |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | Full — Hold |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | OFF | **ON** | Overfill → Drain engaged |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | OFF | OFF | STOP pressed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **Hysteresis Effect** built into Rung 2: once filling starts (LOW = 1), it continues even if LOW momentarily toggles, because the contact $Q_{0.0}$ latches the rung. Filling only stops when HIGH (I0.3) goes true. This is exactly how industrial anti-chatter is achieved.

### 3.4 Step Response of an Analog Level System (For PID Variant)

If a continuous level sensor is used and a PID block (e.g., Siemens FB41 "CONT_C") is enabled, the open-loop step response of the tank alone is a **first-order system**:

$$
G(s) = \frac{H(s)}{Q_{in}(s)} = \frac{R}{\tau \cdot s + 1}
$$

where:

- $R = \dfrac{1}{A}$ — flow resistance reciprocal (m/(m³/s)).
- $\tau = R \cdot A$ — time constant (s).

The PID closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$
\frac{H(s)}{H_{sp}(s)} = \frac{K_{p}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_{d} s\right) \cdot \dfrac{R}{\tau s + 1}}{1 + K_{p}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_{d} s\right) \cdot \dfrac{R}{\tau s + 1}}
$$

Tuning rules (used in labs):

| Method | $K_{p}$ | $T_{i}$ | $T_{d}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ziegler-Nichols (Open Loop) | $\dfrac{\tau}{K \cdot L}$ | $2L$ | $0.5L$ |
| Cohen-Coon | $\dfrac{\tau}{K \cdot L}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{L}{3\tau}\right)$ | $L\!\left(\dfrac{30 + 3L/\tau}{9 + 20L/\tau}\right)$ | $\dfrac{4L}{11 + 2L/\tau}$ |

where $K$ = process gain, $L$ = dead time, $\tau$ = time constant — all obtained from the open-loop step test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Block Diagram — Hardware Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[24V DC Power Supply] --> PSU1[PLC Input Module]
    PSU1 --> PLCproc[PLC CPU Scan Engine]
    PLCproc --> PSU2[PLC Output Module]
    PSU2 --> Relay1[Relay K1 Inlet]
    PSU2 --> Relay2[Relay K2 Outlet]
    Relay1 --> PumpIn[Inlet Pump]
    Relay2 --> PumpOut[Outlet Pump]
    PumpIn --> TankA[Process Tank]
    TankA --> PumpOut
    TankA --> Sens1[LOW Sensor LSL]
    TankA --> Sens2[HIGH Sensor LSH]
    Sens1 --> PSU1
    Sens2 --> PSU1
    PB1[START PB] --> PSU1
    PB2[STOP PB] --> PSU1
    HMI1[Indicator Lamp RUN] --> PSU2
    HMI2[Indicator Lamp FULL] --> PSU2
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Control Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST([Power ON]) --> ChkLSH{High Sensor Active?}
    ChkLSH -- YES --> StopInlet[Inlet OFF]
    ChkLSH -- NO --> ChkLSL{Low Sensor Active?}
    ChkLSL -- YES --> RunInlet[Inlet ON - Fill Mode]
    ChkLSL -- NO --> HoldMode[Hold Mode]
    RunInlet --> SensLoop[Recheck Sensors Every Scan]
    StopInlet --> SensLoop
    HoldMode --> SensLoop
    SensLoop --> ChkLSH
    StopPB[STOP Pressed?] --> StopAll[All Outputs OFF]
    StopAll --> EndSys([System Shutdown])
```

### 4.3 Ladder Logic Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rung1["RUNG 1: Master Latch"]:::rungstyle
    L1[START NO]:::in
    L2[STOP NC]:::in
    L3[RUN Seal-in Contact]:::in
    L4[Coil: M0.0 RUN]:::coil
    
    Rung1 --- L1
    L1 --- L2
    L2 --- L3
    L3 --- L4
    
    Rung2["RUNG 2: Inlet Control"]:::rungstyle
    M1[M0.0 RUN]:::in
    M2[HIGH NC I0.3]:::in
    M3[LOW NO I0.2 OR Seal Q0.0]:::in
    M4[Coil Q0.0 Inlet]:::coil
    
    Rung2 --- M1
    M1 --- M2
    M2 --- M3
    M3 --- M4
    
    Rung3["RUNG 3: Outlet Control"]:::rungstyle
    N1[M0.0 RUN]:::in
    N2[HIGH NO I0.3]:::in
    N3[LOW NC I0.2]:::in
    N4[Coil Q0.1 Outlet]:::coil
    
    Rung3 --- N1
    N1 --- N2
    N2 --- N3
    N3 --- N4
    
    classDef rungstyle fill:#f0f4ff,stroke:#1a3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a3a8a
    classDef in fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef coil fill:#ffe9b3,stroke:#a06000,stroke-width:1px,color:#5a3500
```

### 4.4 Timing Diagram — Sequence of Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant LSL as LOW Sensor
    participant LSH as HIGH Sensor
    participant PLC as PLC CPU
    participant IN as Inlet Pump Q0.0
    participant OUT as Outlet Pump Q0.1
    Note over LSL,OUT: Initial State: Tank Empty
    LSL->>PLC: 0 (No contact)
    LSH->>PLC: 0
    PLC->>IN: Energize Q0.0 ON
    Note over IN: Filling Begins
    LSL->>PLC: 1 (Level reached LOW mark)
    PLC->>IN: Maintain Q0.0 ON (Hysteresis)
    LSH->>PLC: 1 (Level reached HIGH mark)
    PLC->>IN: De-energize Q0.0 OFF
    Note over IN: Filling Stops
    LSL->>PLC: 1
    PLC->>OUT: Maintain Q0.1 OFF
    LSL->>PLC: 0 (Level drops below LOW)
    Note over LSL,IN: Cycle Repeats
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**  
Define the term **Hysteresis Band** in the context of PLC-based two-point level control. Why is it necessary?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Hysteresis Band is the deliberate gap maintained between the LOW setpoint and HIGH setpoint of a level sensor system, defined as $\Delta h = h_{HIGH} - h_{LOW}$. It is necessary because, in a two-point ON/OFF controller, the level oscillates rapidly around a single threshold, causing relay **chattering** (continuous ON/OFF cycling), which reduces the lifespan of contactors and solenoid valves. By introducing a hysteresis gap, the controller commits to one action (e.g., fill) until the level crosses the opposite threshold, providing a **dead-band** that ensures stable, reliable switching.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**  
List any **four** level-sensing methods commonly interfaced with a PLC and state one industrial application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Sensor Type | Working Principle | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Float Switch | Mechanical buoyant contact closure | Sump pump control |
| 2 | Conductive Probe | Electrode pair completes circuit through liquid | Boiler water level |
| 3 | Capacitive | Change in capacitance due to dielectric shift | Chemical tank (oily liquids) |
| 4 | Ultrasonic | Time-of-flight of acoustic echo | Open channel / river gauging |
| 5 | Hydrostatic (Analog) | Pressure at tank bottom proportional to head | Oil storage tanks |

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) regulation, Module-2 questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice between Question A and Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 2 Choice – Set 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat ladder logic diagram and I/O assignment table, explain how a PLC is used to **automatically maintain** the water level in an overhead tank between two setpoints (LOW and HIGH). Clearly mention the role of the seal-in contact.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the dynamic mathematical model of a single-tank level system starting from conservation of mass. State clearly the assumption of **constant cross-sectional area**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks — Stepwise Model Answer]**

1. **I/O Assignment Table (2 marks):**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START PB (NO) | Input |
| I0.1 | STOP PB (NC) | Input |
| I0.2 | LSL (LOW Sensor, NO) | Input |
| I0.3 | LSH (HIGH Sensor, NO) | Input |
| Q0.0 | Inlet Solenoid Valve | Output |
| M0.0 | RUN Latch Coil | Internal |

2. **Ladder Program (3 marks):**

   **Rung 1 (Latch):** `START (NO) OR M0.0 (seal) — series — STOP (NC) — Coil M0.0`  
   **Rung 2 (Inlet):** `M0.0 — series — NOT I0.3 — parallel(I0.2 OR Q0.0) — Coil Q0.0`  
   **Rung 3 (Indicator):** `M0.0 AND I0.3 — Coil FULL_LAMP`

3. **Role of Seal-in Contact (2 marks):** The seal-in contact (parallel branch around the START button) maintains the RUN state after the operator releases START. It is broken only when STOP is pressed OR a safety condition occurs (e.g., LSH fault). Without the seal-in contact, the system would require continuous button pressing — impractical for level control.

**(b) [7 Marks — Stepwise Model Answer]**

1. Start with the **principle of conservation of mass**: rate of mass inflow − rate of mass outflow = rate of mass accumulation. **(1 mark)**

2. For an **incompressible liquid** (ρ = constant), substitute $\dot{m} = \rho Q$: **(1 mark)**

   $$\rho \frac{dV}{dt} = \rho Q_{in} - \rho Q_{out}$$

3. For a tank with **constant cross-sectional area A**, the volume is $V = A \cdot h$, so $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt}$. **(1.5 marks)**

4. Cancel ρ on both sides: **(0.5 mark)**

   $$\boxed{\,A \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}\,}$$

5. **Assumption stated explicitly (1.5 marks):** Constant cross-sectional area means $A$ does not vary with height $h$ — valid for **cylindrical vertical tanks**. For conical or hemispherical tanks, $A(h)$ must be retained inside the derivative.

6. **Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
   - $Q_{in} > Q_{out}$ → level rises.
   - $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$ → steady state.
   - $Q_{in} < Q_{out}$ → level falls.
   - This equation is the basis for tuning PID controllers in continuous-level applications.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *INTERNAL CHOICE*

**[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 2 Choice – Set 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **two-point ON/OFF control** with **continuous PID control** for tank level applications. Mention at least **five** points of comparison.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a ladder logic program for **three-point sequential control** of a tank with LOW, MID, and HIGH sensors such that the inlet valve opens when level falls below LOW and the outlet valve opens only when level exceeds HIGH. The MID sensor activates a **warning lamp**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Two-Point (ON/OFF) | PID Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensors required | 2 digital (LOW, HIGH) | 1 analog (4–20 mA) |
| 2 | Output type | Discrete (ON/OFF) | Continuous (0–100% valve position / VFD speed) |
| 3 | Steady-state error | Always present (offset) | Zero (with integral action) |
| 4 | Oscillation around setpoint | Yes, within hysteresis band | Minimal (tuned) |
| 5 | Actuator wear | Higher (frequent switching) | Lower (smooth modulation) |
| 6 | Programming complexity | Simple ladder | Requires FB41 / PID instruction |
| 7 | Cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| 8 | Typical use | Domestic overhead tanks | Chemical process reactors |

**(b) [7 Marks — Ladder Program for 3-Point Control]**

I/O Assignment:

| Address | Device |
|---|---|
| I0.0 | START |
| I0.1 | STOP |
| I0.2 | LOW Sensor (LSL) |
| I0.3 | MID Sensor (LSM) |
| I0.4 | HIGH Sensor (LSH) |
| Q0.0 | Inlet Valve |
| Q0.1 | Outlet Valve |
| Q0.2 | Warning Lamp |

**Rung 1 — Master Latch:** `(START OR M0.0) AND NOT STOP → M0.0`

**Rung 2 — Inlet Valve Control:**  
`M0.0 AND NOT I0.4 AND (NOT I0.2 OR Q0.0) → Q0.0`  
Meaning: Inlet ON when system RUN, level not HIGH, and (level is at/below LOW OR inlet already latched).

**Rung 3 — Warning Lamp:**  
`M0.0 AND I0.3 → Q0.2`  
Meaning: When MID sensor detects level, lamp turns ON — operator alert.

**Rung 4 — Outlet Valve:**  
`M0.0 AND I0.4 AND NOT I0.2 → Q0.1`  
Meaning: Drain only when level is above HIGH and tank is NOT empty (safety to prevent dry-pump damage).

**Rung 5 — Audible Buzzer (overfill alarm):**  
`M0.0 AND I0.4 AND I0.3 → BUZZER`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting NC/NO distinction** for STOP button. STOP must always be wired NC so that a wire-break fails SAFE (system shuts down).
> 2. **Missing seal-in (latching) contact** around START — students often write START alone, which would not retain the RUN state.
> 3. **Reversing I0.2 and I0.4 logic** — forgetting that LSL is at the bottom (turns ON when liquid reaches it from below), while LSH is at the top.
> 4. **Ignoring hysteresis** — directly connecting the LOW sensor to energize the inlet without latching causes **relay chattering** when liquid surface ripples.
> 5. **In analog/PID part**, students often forget to **scale the 4–20 mA signal** to engineering units (e.g., 0–100%) before using it as PID PV — losing up to **2 marks**.
> 6. **Not stating the incompressibility assumption** in the derivation — examiners deduct **1 mark** specifically for this.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core principle:** PLC scans inputs every **≤ 10 ms** and decides outputs based on ladder logic — true level control is a **discrete-event feedback system**.
- **Three components are mandatory** in every answer: (i) **Sensor** (LSL / LSH / analog), (ii) **Decision unit** (PLC + program), (iii) **Actuator** (pump / solenoid).
- **Hysteresis band** = $h_{HIGH} - h_{LOW}$ — always mention it when describing two-point control; it eliminates relay chattering.
- **Dynamic model** of the tank: $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt}$. This is the first equation you should write if asked about tank dynamics.
- **Seal-in contact** in ladder logic is the PLC equivalent of a **holding relay** in classical relay logic — without it, the system cannot remember its state between scans.
- **Three industrial sensor families** to remember for viva:
  - **Contact type** → Float switch, conductive probe.
  - **Property-change type** → Capacitive, ultrasonic, radar.
  - **Head-pressure type** → Hydrostatic / DP cell.
- **PID tuning rule of thumb for tanks:** start with $K_{p} = 1$, $T_{i} = \tau$, $T_{d} = 0$ (PI only — derivative is rarely used for level because of noise).
- **Safety priority in ladder logic** — Emergency STOP, OVERFLOW protection, and DRY-RUN protection must be wired in **series** (highest priority) before any start logic.
- **For KTU record submission** — always include: (i) Aim, (ii) Apparatus, (iii) Circuit diagram / I/O list, (iv) Ladder program (printed), (v) Timing diagram, (vi) Result table with actual vs. expected, (vii) Viva questions and answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of sequential switching of motors using PLC
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL607 - MECHATRONICS SYSTEMS & PLC LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of sequential switching of motors using PLC

# 🔌 Simulation of Sequential Switching of Motors Using PLC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Sequential switching of motors using a PLC* is a programmed control strategy in which multiple **three-phase induction motors** (or DC motors) are energized in a pre-determined order, with controlled time delays between successive starts, using the **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** as the central decision-making element. The ladder logic program uses **timer instructions, latching coils, and interlock contacts** to ensure that no two motors start simultaneously and that the sequence follows the exact desired temporal order.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Domino Conveyor Belt"

Imagine a factory conveyor belt where three workers are stationed. A supervisor (the **PLC**) does not shout at all workers at once; instead, he tells Worker-1 to start, waits **5 seconds**, then tells Worker-2 to start, waits another **5 seconds**, then tells Worker-3 to start. Each worker only starts when the previous one is already running. If the supervisor presses **STOP**, all workers halt simultaneously. This is *exactly* what a sequential motor starter does — but using **electrical contacts** instead of verbal commands.

The PLC acts as the "supervisor brain":
- It **reads inputs** (START pushbutton, STOP pushbutton, overload contacts).
- It **executes the ladder logic** line by line (scan cycle).
- It **drives outputs** (Motor contactors M1, M2, M3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The experiment demonstrates **motor interlocking**, **ladder logic programming** using **on-delay timers (TON)**, **latching (seal-in) circuits**, and **simulation in PLC software** (such as **Siemens LOGO! Soft Comfort**, **Allen-Bradley RSLogix 500**, or **Codesys**).

### Key Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PLC Scan Time** | Time taken by the PLC to read all inputs, execute logic, and update outputs (typically **10–50 ms**). |
| **Latching (Seal-in)** | A contactor/relay coil that "remembers" its ON state after the start button is released. |
| **Interlock** | A normally-closed contact of one motor placed in series with another motor's coil to prevent simultaneous starts. |
| **On-Delay Timer (TON)** | Timer that begins counting only when its input rung becomes TRUE; output turns ON after the preset time elapses. |
| **Contactor** | An electromagnetically-controlled switch used to energize high-current motor circuits from a low-current PLC output. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 How Sequential Motor Switching Works — Step-by-Step Logic

A sequential motor switching system is governed by three fundamental PLC concepts working together:

#### **(a) Latching Circuit (Set/Reset Action)**
The **START pushbutton** is a *momentary* input — it is pressed briefly and released. The motor, however, must remain ON until the STOP is pressed. This is achieved by **latching**: a normally-open auxiliary contact of the motor's own output is wired in **parallel** with the START button. Once energized, this auxiliary contact "seals" the circuit, keeping the motor running even after START is released.

#### **(b) On-Delay Timer (TON)**
The **on-delay timer** is the *heart* of the sequential logic. When its input rung becomes TRUE (i.e., when the previous motor is running), the timer begins counting up from **0** to its **preset value**. When the accumulated time equals the preset, the timer's **Done (DN) bit** turns ON, which becomes the trigger for the next motor.

#### **(c) Interlock (Safety Logic)**
An **interlock** is a normally-closed contact of one motor placed in series with another motor's coil. This prevents two motors from starting simultaneously and also prevents the second motor from starting if the first is not running (even if the timer expires).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is sequencing important?**
> - **Reduces inrush current**: Starting all motors at once causes a massive current spike that can trip circuit breakers.
> - **Prevents mechanical shock** to coupled machinery.
> - **Required in process industries** like cement plants, sugar mills, and bottling plants where conveyors must start in a specific order.

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Sequence Completion Time | $T_{total} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \dots + t_n$ | **seconds (s)** | Sum of all inter-motor delays |
| 2 | Single Timer Preset | $T_{preset} = t_{delay}$ | **seconds (s)** | Time gap between two consecutive motor starts |
| 3 | Effective Scan Cycle | $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{logic} + T_{output}$ | **milliseconds (ms)** | Typical value: **10–50 ms** |
| 4 | Number of Program Scans during Delay | $N_{scans} = \dfrac{T_{preset}}{T_{scan}}$ | **dimensionless** | Shows how many times the ladder is executed during one timer period |
| 5 | Inrush Current Reduction Factor | $I_{sequential} = \dfrac{I_{full-load}}{n}$ | **Amperes (A)** | Where $n$ = number of motors running at once (here, sequential = 1) |
| 6 | Motor Power Equation (3-phase) | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | **Watts (W)** | Used when calculating load characteristics |
| 7 | Counter Capacity Check | $N_{max} = \dfrac{C_{timer}}{T_{scan}}$ | **dimensionless** | Max timers usable without scan overrun |

> [!NOTE]
> In most KTU lab examinations, students are expected to use the **timer preset formula** $T_{preset} = t_{delay}$ and the **total sequence time formula** $T_{total} = \sum t_i$ directly. The scan time and inrush equations are bonus marks for Part-B questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📝 Worked Example: 3-Motor Sequential Start with 5-Second Delays

**Problem Statement (KTU Lab Style):**
*Design a PLC ladder logic program to start three motors M1, M2, M3 in sequence. M1 must start immediately when START is pressed. M2 must start 5 seconds after M1. M3 must start 5 seconds after M2. Pressing STOP must switch off all motors immediately. An overload relay contact (OL) must stop the system in case of any motor fault.*

#### **Step 1: I/O Address Allocation**

| Symbol | Address | Type | Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| **START** | **I0.0** | Input (NO) | Pushbutton (Green) |
| **STOP** | **I0.1** | Input (NC) | Pushbutton (Red) |
| **OL** | **I0.2** | Input (NC) | Overload Relay Contact |
| **M1** | **Q0.0** | Output | Motor 1 Contactor |
| **M2** | **Q0.1** | Output | Motor 2 Contactor |
| **M3** | **Q0.2** | Output | Motor 3 Contactor |
| **T1** | T37 | Timer (TON) | 5-second timer |
| **T2** | T38 | Timer (TON) | 5-second timer |

#### **Step 2: Ladder Logic Derivation — Rung-by-Rung**

**RUNG 1 — Motor 1 Latch Circuit:**

$$\text{Rung 1: } \underbrace{\left[ \text{START} \, (I0.0) \parallel M1 \, (Q0.0) \right]}_{\text{Parallel OR — Latch logic}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\overline{\text{STOP}} \, (I0.1) \rightarrow \overline{\text{OL}} \, (I0.2)}_{\text{Series NC — Safety cut-off}} \rightarrow \underbrace{M1 \, (Q0.0)}_{\text{Output Coil}}$$

*Explanation:* START OR the seal-in contact of M1 keeps the rung TRUE; STOP and OL (both normally-closed) must be closed for current to flow to coil M1.

**RUNG 2 — Timer 1 (5-second delay before M2):**

$$\text{Rung 2: } \underbrace{M1 \, (Q0.0)}_{\text{Trigger source}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{TON} \, T37, \, \text{Preset} = 5\text{s}}_{\text{On-delay timer block}}$$

*Explanation:* As soon as M1 energizes, Timer T37 starts counting. After 5 seconds, bit **T37.DN** (Done) turns ON.

**RUNG 3 — Motor 2 Latch with Interlock:**

$$\text{Rung 3: } \underbrace{\left[ T37.DN \parallel M2 \right]}_{\text{Latch}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\overline{\text{STOP}} \rightarrow \overline{\text{OL}}}_{\text{Safety}} \rightarrow \underbrace{M2 \, (Q0.1)}_{\text{Output Coil}}$$

*Explanation:* M2 can only latch when Timer T37 reports DONE (i.e., 5 s after M1 started). M2 also latches itself via its own seal-in contact.

**RUNG 4 — Timer 2 (5-second delay before M3):**

$$\text{Rung 4: } \underbrace{M2 \, (Q0.1)}_{\text{Trigger source}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{TON} \, T38, \, \text{Preset} = 5\text{s}}_{\text{Second on-delay timer}}$$

**RUNG 5 — Motor 3 Latch:**

$$\text{Rung 5: } \underbrace{\left[ T38.DN \parallel M3 \right]}_{\text{Latch}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\overline{\text{STOP}} \rightarrow \overline{\text{OL}}}_{\text{Safety}} \rightarrow \underbrace{M3 \, (Q0.2)}_{\text{Output Coil}}$$

#### **Step 3: Total Sequence Time Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= t_{M1 \rightarrow M2} + t_{M2 \rightarrow M3} \\
T_{total} &= T_{preset, T37} + T_{preset, T38} \\
T_{total} &= 5\text{ s} + 5\text{ s} \\
T_{total} &= 10\text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** M1 itself starts at **t = 0 s**, M2 at **t = 5 s**, and M3 at **t = 10 s**. All three motors stop simultaneously when STOP is pressed (because the latching path is broken for all three at once).

#### **Step 4: Number of PLC Scans During Delay**

Assuming a typical PLC scan time of $T_{scan} = 20$ ms $= 0.02$ s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{scans} &= \frac{T_{preset}}{T_{scan}} \\
N_{scans} &= \frac{5\text{ s}}{0.02\text{ s}} \\
N_{scans} &= 250 \text{ scans per timer}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the ladder logic executes **250 times** during the 5-second delay, demonstrating how reliable modern PLC timing is.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔧 Diagram A — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

This diagram illustrates the **input → PLC → output** signal flow of the sequential motor control system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT DEVICES"]
        A1[START Pushbutton NO - I0.0]
        A2[STOP Pushbutton NC - I0.1]
        A3[Overload Relay NC - I0.2]
    end

    subgraph PLC["PLC CENTRAL UNIT"]
        B1[Input Module - 24V DC]
        B2[CPU Scan Cycle]
        B3[Ladder Logic Memory]
        B4[Output Module - Relay]
    end

    subgraph TIMERS["TIMER BANK"]
        C1[T37 - TON 5 sec]
        C2[T38 - TON 5 sec]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT DEVICES"]
        D1[Contactor K1 - Motor M1]
        D2[Contactor K2 - Motor M2]
        D3[Contactor K3 - Motor M3]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> B4
    B4 --> D1
    B4 --> D2
    B4 --> D3
    D1 -.seal-in feedback.-> B3
    D2 -.seal-in feedback.-> B3
    D3 -.seal-in feedback.-> B3
```

### 🔁 Diagram B — Sequential Motor Start State Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> M1_Running : START pressed
    M1_Running --> M2_Running : T37 done after 5 sec
    M2_Running --> M3_Running : T38 done after 5 sec
    M3_Running --> All_Running : All three motors ON
    All_Running --> Idle : STOP pressed
    M1_Running --> Fault : OL trip
    M2_Running --> Fault : OL trip
    M3_Running --> Fault : OL trip
    Fault --> Idle : Reset OL
```

### 🪜 Diagram C — Ladder Logic Functional Flow (Rung-by-Rung)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["RUNG 1: M1 Latch - START OR M1 seals M1 ON"]
    R2["RUNG 2: T37 starts when M1 ON, preset 5 sec"]
    R3["RUNG 3: M2 Latch - T37.DN OR M2 seals M2 ON"]
    R4["RUNG 4: T38 starts when M2 ON, preset 5 sec"]
    R5["RUNG 5: M3 Latch - T38.DN OR M3 seals M3 ON"]
    R6["RUNG 6: END"]

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardware Wiring Note (Practical Lab):**
> The PLC output contacts (Q0.0, Q0.1, Q0.2) are **low-power** signals (typically 24 V DC, 0.5 A). They cannot directly drive motor contactor coils (which may need 230 V AC). Hence, the PLC output energizes an **interposing relay or contactor coil**, whose heavy-duty contacts then switch the **three-phase supply** to the motor.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. What is a latching (seal-in) circuit in a PLC? Why is it necessary in motor control?**

**Model Answer:**
A latching or seal-in circuit is a parallel connection of an auxiliary normally-open contact of an output coil with the momentary START pushbutton. Once the START button energizes the coil, the auxiliary contact closes and keeps the coil energized even after START is released. **It is necessary because START pushbuttons are momentary** — they return to OFF state when released. Without latching, the motor would start and immediately stop the moment the operator releases the button. The circuit is broken only when the STOP (NC) button is pressed or when the overload relay trips.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between an on-delay timer (TON) and an off-delay timer (TOF) in the context of sequential motor starting.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **TON (On-Delay)** | **TOF (Off-Delay)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Starts when input turns **ON** | Starts when input turns **OFF** |
| **Output behavior** | Output turns ON **after** preset time | Output turns OFF **after** preset time |
| **Use in motor seq.** | Delays the **start** of the next motor | Delays the **stop** of the previous motor |
| **Reset** | Resets immediately when input goes FALSE | Resets immediately when input goes TRUE |

For sequential motor starting, **TON is preferred** because we want each subsequent motor to start only after a fixed delay following the previous motor.

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

---

#### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Design a PLC ladder logic program to start three motors **M1, M2, M3** in sequence with a **10-second delay** between each. Provide the complete I/O address assignment and explain the role of each rung. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory uses this sequential starter. Calculate the total time for full sequence completion. If the PLC scan time is **15 ms**, determine how many scans occur during the second delay interval. Comment on what happens if motor M2's contactor fails to close. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — I/O Assignment & Ladder Logic Design:**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START (NO) | Input |
| I0.1 | STOP (NC) | Input |
| I0.2 | Overload (NC) | Input |
| Q0.0 | Motor 1 Contactor | Output |
| Q0.1 | Motor 2 Contactor | Output |
| Q0.2 | Motor 3 Contactor | Output |
| T37 | TON, 10 s | Internal Timer |
| T38 | TON, 10 s | Internal Timer |

**Rung-by-Rung Explanation:**

- **Rung 1:** `M1 = (START ∥ M1) · \overline{STOP} · \overline{OL}` → Latching circuit for M1.
- **Rung 2:** `T37 = TON(M1, Preset = 10 s)` → Timer begins counting when M1 is ON.
- **Rung 3:** `M2 = (T37.DN ∥ M2) · \overline{STOP} · \overline{OL}` → M2 latches only after 10 s.
- **Rung 4:** `T38 = TON(M2, Preset = 10 s)` → Second timer for M3 delay.
- **Rung 5:** `M3 = (T38.DN ∥ M3) · \overline{STOP} · \overline{OL}` → M3 latches after another 10 s.

**(b) — Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= t_{M1 \rightarrow M2} + t_{M2 \rightarrow M3} \\
&= 10\text{ s} + 10\text{ s} \\
&= 20\text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{scans} &= \frac{T_{preset}}{T_{scan}} = \frac{10\text{ s}}{0.015\text{ s}} \\
N_{scans} &= 666.67 \approx 667 \text{ scans}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on M2 contactor failure:** If M2's contactor fails to close, the auxiliary contact of M2 will not establish, so **T38 will never start counting** (because its trigger is M2's Q0.1 output). Consequently, **M3 will never start**, and the system will be in a *partial-running state* with M1 ON, M2 OFF, and M3 OFF. The operator must press STOP, troubleshoot the contactor, and restart.

---

#### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the concept of **motor interlocking** using a PLC. How does it differ from a conventional relay-based interlock? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a ladder logic program for **reversing the direction** of a single 3-phase induction motor (Forward / Reverse) using a PLC, with **electrical and software interlocks**. Provide the I/O list. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Motor Interlocking:**
Motor interlocking ensures that two (or more) mutually exclusive operations cannot occur simultaneously. In PLC-based interlocking, a **normally-closed contact** of one motor's output is placed in **series** with the other motor's coil in the ladder logic. This is purely a **software interlock** — no extra hardware wiring needed. In contrast, **conventional relay-based interlocking** requires physically wiring the auxiliary NC contacts of one contactor in series with the other contactor's coil, which is bulky, harder to modify, and prone to contact welding failures.

**(b) Forward/Reverse Ladder Logic (Key Idea):**

| Address | Device |
|---|---|
| I0.0 | STOP (NC) |
| I0.1 | FORWARD Pushbutton (NO) |
| I0.2 | REVERSE Pushbutton (NO) |
| Q0.0 | Forward Contactor (F) |
| Q0.1 | Reverse Contactor (R) |

Rungs:
- F-coil rung: `F = (FORWARD ∥ F) · \overline{STOP} · \overline{R}`  ← software interlock via R's NC contact
- R-coil rung: `R = (REVERSE ∥ R) · \overline{STOP} · \overline{F}`  ← software interlock via F's NC contact

This ensures **F and R can never be ON together**, preventing a phase-to-phase short circuit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the STOP and Overload NC contacts** in series with every motor coil — this costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Not drawing the seal-in (parallel) contact** of each motor coil — examiners specifically check for the latch.
> 3. **Triggering Timer T2 from Q0.1 but latching M3 from T2.DN** — students often confuse the trigger source.
> 4. **Failing to mention hardware interlock** (in addition to software) for forward/reverse motor questions — the KTU answer key demands *both*.
> 5. **Skipping I/O address tables** — without an I/O table, marks are deducted even if the logic is correct.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Sequential motor starting** uses **TON timers** to introduce **time delays** between successive motor energizations.
- 🔹 The **latching (seal-in) contact** is a **parallel auxiliary contact** of the motor's own output coil.
- 🔹 The **interlock** is a **series NC contact** of one motor in the rung of another motor.
- 🔹 **Total sequence time** $T_{total} = \sum t_i$ — sum of all timer presets.
- 🔹 **PLC scan time** is typically **10–50 ms** — must be much smaller than the timer preset for accurate timing.
- 🔹 **Inputs** to PLC: **START (NO), STOP (NC), Overload (NC)**.
- 🔹 **Outputs** from PLC: **Motor contactors (M1, M2, M3)** — driven via interposing relays.
- 🔹 **Hardware vs. software interlock**: PLC software interlock (NC contact in ladder) is more reliable than relay-based hardware interlock because it has no moving parts.
- 🔹 **Reset condition**: STOP button or Overload trip **de-energizes ALL motors simultaneously** because it breaks the series path for every rung.
- 🔹 **Always include the I/O address allocation table** at the top of your answer — KTU examiners expect it.
- 🔹 For **forward-reverse motor control**, **both electrical and software interlocks** are mandatory to prevent short circuits.
